## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

> **"Define the term 'Enlightened View of Manufacturing'. How does it differ from the traditional view?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Enlightened View of Manufacturing** is a strategic management perspective that recognizes manufacturing as a **strategic resource** capable of creating competitive advantage, rather than merely a cost center. It was pioneered by **Wickham Skinner**.

**Key differences:**

| **Aspect** | **Traditional View** | **Enlightened View** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Manufacturing role | Cost center | Strategic resource |
| Performance measure | Internal cost | Customer-relevant criteria |
| Strategic participation | Subordinate | Equal partner |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison = 3 marks)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

> **"Distinguish between Order Winners and Order Qualifiers with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Order Qualifiers (OQ):** Minimum criteria a product *must satisfy* to even be considered by the customer. *Example:* An automobile must have basic safety features (airbags, ABS) — these are qualifiers, not differentiators.
- **Order Winners (OW):** Criteria that *actually secure* the customer's purchase decision against competing alternatives. *Example:* For Tesla, range per charge and autonomous driving are order winners.

A criterion can shift from $OQ$ to $OW$ over time (e.g., fuel efficiency was an $OW$ in the 1970s oil crisis but is now an $OQ$ for most markets).

*(1.5 marks each concept + 0.5 marks for the shift note = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **five competitive priorities** of manufacturing strategy. How does a firm choose among them? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **Skinner's Focused Factory** concept. How does it resolve the inherent trade-offs in manufacturing? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The **five competitive priorities** are the strategic dimensions on which a manufacturing function can compete:

1. **Cost** — Ability to produce at the lowest unit cost. Compete on price.
2. **Quality** — Conformance to specifications (manufacturing quality) + meeting customer needs (product quality).
3. **Delivery** — Speed (lead time) + reliability (on-time performance).
4. **Flexibility** — Volume flexibility + mix flexibility + product flexibility.
5. **Innovation** — Ability to introduce new products/processes rapidly.

**How firms choose among them:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1:} \quad \text{Identify customer-relevant criteria (Order Winners and Qualifiers).} \\
&\text{Step 2:} \quad \text{Map competitor capabilities.} \\
&\text{Step 3:} \quad \text{Select 2 to 3 priorities as primary, treating others as qualifiers.} \\
&\text{Step 4:} \quad \text{Ensure chosen priorities align with corporate strategy.} \\
&\text{Step 5:} \quad \text{Validate using Strategic Fit equation: } SF \to 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Real-world example:** Toyota competes on **Cost + Quality + Delivery Reliability** (the "3 Cs") — but not on rapid innovation or extreme customization. This focused choice is deliberate.

*(2 marks enumeration + 3 marks selection process + 2 marks example = 7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Skinner's Focused Factory** concept (1974) argues that a plant attempting to excel on **all** competitive priorities simultaneously will inevitably fail because of inherent **internal trade-offs**.

**Core argument:**

$$
\text{Plant trying to be "all things to all people"} \Rightarrow \text{Mediocre on every priority.}
$$

**The Focused Factory Solution:**

- Each plant is dedicated to a **limited product-market set**.
- Each plant has its **own manufacturing mission** aligned to a specific competitive priority bundle.
- Trade-offs are **managed internally** rather than ignored.

**How it resolves trade-offs:**

By splitting the multi-product factory into **multiple focused factories**, each plant operates on a different point of its own PPF. The corporate entity gains access to a wider competitive envelope than any single plant could achieve.

**Example:** A conglomerate with three plants — Plant A focuses on high-volume/low-cost, Plant B on high-mix/low-volume, Plant C on premium-quality/low-volume. Each excels; the conglomerate as a whole serves diverse markets.

*(2 marks concept + 2 marks argument + 3 marks resolution + example = 7 marks)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** Describe the **three-tier hierarchy of manufacturing decisions** (structural, infrastructural, operational). Why is this hierarchy critical to strategic alignment? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Production Possibility Frontier (PPF)** in the context of manufacturing trade-offs. Using a suitable diagram, illustrate why a firm cannot simultaneously maximize quality and minimize cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

| **Tier** | **Decision Type** | **Examples** | **Time Horizon** | **Reversibility** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tier 1 | Structural | Capacity, facility location, technology, vertical integration | 5–10 years | Very low (sunk cost) |
| Tier 2 | Infrastructural | Quality systems, workforce policies, planning, organization | 2–5 years | Medium |
| Tier 3 | Operational | Scheduling, inventory levels, shift patterns | Daily–Quarterly | High |

**Why this hierarchy matters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Cascade Logic:} \quad \text{Tier 1 decisions constrain Tier 2; Tier 2 constrains Tier 3.} \\
&\textbf{Alignment Rule:} \quad \text{Lower-tier decisions must SUPPORT higher-tier decisions.}
\end{aligned}
$$

If a firm adopts a **cost-leadership** corporate strategy (Tier 1 = high-volume automated plant), then Tier 2 must enforce tight cost-control processes, and Tier 3 must prioritize throughput over variety. **Any contradiction across tiers creates strategic misalignment.**

*(2 marks table + 2 marks cascade logic + 3 marks alignment rule + example = 7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

The **Production Possibility Frontier (PPF)** represents the **maximum achievable combination** of two competing objectives given a fixed technology and resource base.

**Mathematical form (cost vs. quality):**

$$
C_{min} = a + b \cdot e^{-\lambda Q}
$$

**Trade-off logic:**

$$
\frac{dC}{dQ} = -b\lambda e^{-\lambda Q} < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Improving quality ALWAYS requires increased cost.}
$$

**Why a firm cannot simultaneously maximize both:**

1. **Physical constraint:** Higher quality requires better materials, tighter tolerances, more inspection time — all of which increase cost.
2. **Technological constraint:** Process technology optimized for high speed/low cost (e.g., transfer lines) sacrifices flexibility for quality.
3. **Human resource constraint:** Skilled labor required for premium quality is costlier than unskilled labor.

**Diagrammatic illustration:**

```
Cost ($)
  ^
  |  *
  |    *
  |      *  ← PPF (Convex Downward)
  |        *
  |          *
  |            *
  |              *
  |________________*→ Quality Index (Q)
         Infeasible Region (above curve)
```

The shaded region **above the PPF is infeasible** — no amount of effort can achieve both highest quality AND lowest cost simultaneously. The firm's strategic choice is **which point on the PPF to occupy**.

*(2 marks definition + 2 marks math/logic + 3 marks constraint explanation + diagram = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Order Winner" with "Order Qualifier"** — Many students write them as synonyms. They are *distinct*: Qualifiers are necessary (table stakes); Winners are decisive (the differentiator).
> 2. **Listing all five competitive priorities as if all are equally pursued** — A correct answer MUST show that the firm **chooses** among them; the PPF concept exists precisely because *focus* is required.
> 3. **Skipping the trade-off logic** — Any question on the enlightened view that does not explicitly mention **trade-offs** will lose 2–3 marks. Trade-offs are the *heart* of the theory.
> 4. **Ignoring the Skinner/Historical context** — KTU examiners expect attribution to **Wickham Skinner (1969, 1974)** and acknowledgment of the 1970s–80s competitive crisis that motivated the enlightened view.
> 5. **Treating manufacturing as only a cost center in answers** — This is precisely what the enlightened view *rejects*. Any answer that frames manufacturing purely in cost terms contradicts the question's premise and will be heavily penalized.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Enlightened View of Manufacturing**
>
> ✅ **Originator:** Wickham Skinner (Harvard, 1969, 1974); extended by Hayes, Wheelwright, Hill.
>
> ✅ **Core Definition:** Manufacturing is a **strategic resource and competitive weapon**, not merely a cost center.
>
> ✅ **Five Competitive Priorities (C-Q-D-F-I):** Cost, Quality, Delivery, Flexibility, Innovation.
>
> ✅ **Order Winners (OW):** Criteria that *win* the order — the differentiator.
>
> ✅ **Order Qualifiers (OQ):** Minimum thresholds to be considered — the table stakes.
>
> ✅ **Production Possibility Frontier (PPF):** Convex curve showing maximum achievable combinations of two objectives; trade-offs are inherent and unavoidable.
>
> ✅ **Three Decision Tiers:** Structural (5–10 yrs) → Infrastructural (2–5 yrs) → Operational (daily-quarterly).
>
> ✅ **Strategic Fit ($SF$):** Degree of alignment between manufacturing mission $M_m$ and corporate strategy $S_c$; ideal value is $SF = 1$.
>
> ✅ **Focused Factory (Skinner):** Each plant should have a **limited product-market focus** to manage trade-offs internally.
>
> ✅ **Three Pillars of the Enlightened View:** Strategic Content + Decision Hierarchy + Trade-off Recognition.
>
> ✅ **Historical Trigger:** 1970s American manufacturing decline vs. Japanese rise — exposed the inadequacy of cost-only thinking.
>
> ✅ **Key Equation to Memorize:** $C_{min} = a + b \cdot e^{-\lambda Q}$ — illustrates the convex PPF trade-off between cost and quality.
>
> ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Confusing $OW$ with $OQ$; omitting trade-off discussion; treating manufacturing as a cost center.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Anchor:** Toyota's 3Cs (Cost, Quality, Consistency) — a live demonstration of focused competitive priority selection.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT866 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management and Manufacturing Excellence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Total Quality Management (TQM) — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a **comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy** that seeks to embed **quality in every functional area, process, and employee activity** to deliver superior value to customers through **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** and **prevention-based defect elimination**.

According to the **American Society for Quality (ASQ)**, TQM is a *management approach to long-term success through customer satisfaction*, achieved by having all members of an organization — from top management to shop-floor workers — participate in improving products, services, and the culture in which they work.

### Core Pillars of TQM (KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Highlights)

| Pillar | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Total** | Quality responsibility extends to **every employee, every department, every process** |
| **Quality** | Degree to which a product/service meets **customer requirements** and **fitness for use** |
| **Management** | The act of **planning, organizing, leading, and controlling** quality efforts strategically |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Focus (Dec 2023 / June 2024 Trend):** Examiners frequently ask for the *difference between TQM and traditional quality control*, and the *eight universally accepted principles of TQM (ISO 9000:2015 aligned)*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — TQM as a Restaurant Chain 🍽️

Imagine a famous restaurant chain — say, a global 5-star hotel. Why does every outlet in Tokyo, Paris, and Kerala serve **identical tasting dishes**? It is **NOT** because one chef controls everything. It is because:

- **The Recipe Book (Standards/SOPs)** is standardized worldwide.
- **The Ingredients (Supplier Quality)** are audited and pre-approved.
- **The Chefs (Employees)** are trained, empowered, and given authority to halt service if quality drops.
- **Customer Feedback (Voice of Customer — VOC)** is collected after every meal.
- **Every Outlet Manager (Leadership)** sets quality goals and reviews them weekly.

This is **TQM in action**. In a factory, the "recipe book" is the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)**, the "ingredients" are **incoming raw materials**, the "chefs" are the **operators**, and the "customer feedback" is **after-sales data**.

---

## 1.3 Manufacturing Excellence — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Excellence** is the *systematic, sustained, and holistic achievement of world-class performance* across the **four pillars of operations**: **Quality, Cost, Delivery, and Safety (QCDS)** — supported by **Lean, Six Sigma, and Digital Transformation**.

The **EFQM Excellence Model** (European Foundation for Quality Management) and the **Baldrige Criteria** (USA — Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award) are the two globally accepted frameworks used to benchmark Manufacturing Excellence.

### Five Pillars of Manufacturing Excellence (Industry 4.0 Aligned)

1. **Operational Excellence (OpEx)** — Lean + Six Sigma
2. **Quality Excellence** — Zero-Defect mindset
3. **Cost Excellence** — Waste elimination, value engineering
4. **Delivery Excellence** — On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)
5. **People Excellence** — Skill development, engagement, Kaizen culture

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Manufacturing Excellence is **NOT** just TQM. It is TQM + Lean + Six Sigma + Industry 4.0 + Sustainability, integrated as a single strategic framework.

---

## 1.4 Evolution of Quality Thinking (Historical Context — Frequently Asked)

| Era | Philosophy | Quality Gurus |
|---|---|---|
| **1920s–30s** | Inspection (Detect defects) | Shewhart (Control Charts) |
| **1950s–60s** | Quality Control (Statistical QC) | Deming, Juran, Feigenbaum |
| **1970s–80s** | Quality Assurance (System-based) | Ishikawa (Fishbone), Taguchi (Loss Function) |
| **1980s–90s** | TQM (Total approach) | Crosby (Zero Defects), Imai (Kaizen) |
| **1990s–2000s** | Six Sigma (DMAIC) | Motorola, GE (Jack Welch) |
| **2000s–Today** | Lean Six Sigma + Digital Quality | Womack, Toyota Production System |
| **2020s+** | Industry 4.0 + Smart Quality + ESG | AI-driven predictive quality |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Principles of TQM (ISO 9000:2015 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These eight principles are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU Module 3 — questions worth up to **14 marks** are routinely framed around them.

1. **Customer Focus** — The primary goal is to *meet and exceed* customer expectations. Identify customer needs via VOC, surveys, QFD.
2. **Leadership** — Leaders establish *unity of purpose, direction, and engagement*. They create an environment where people can excel.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered, and engaged people at *all levels* are the essence of the organization.
4. **Process Approach** — Desired results are achieved more efficiently when activities are understood and managed as **interrelated processes**.
5. **Improvement** — Successful organizations have an *ongoing, continuous* focus on improvement (Kaizen).
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on the *analysis and evaluation of data* are more likely to produce desired results.
7. **Relationship Management** — For sustained success, organizations must manage *relationships with interested parties*, especially suppliers.
8. **Systematic Approach to Management** — Identifying, understanding, and managing *interrelated processes* as a system contributes to effectiveness.

---

## 2.2 TQM Implementation Framework (KTU Analytical View)

TQM implementation follows the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle** by **Deming**, also known as the **Deming Wheel**.

| Phase | Activity |
|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Identify the problem, analyze the process, establish objectives & metrics |
| **DO** | Implement the planned action on a pilot basis |
| **CHECK** | Measure results against expected outcomes, validate with data |
| **ACT** | Standardize the improvement if successful; otherwise, refine and repeat |

> [!NOTE]
> The **PDCA cycle is iterative and continuous**. A factory that runs one PDCA cycle per quarter can expect **15–25% reduction in defects** per cycle.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Most students confuse **Cp with Cpk**. Cp measures *potential* capability; Cpk measures *actual* (centered) capability. Both can appear in 14-mark numerical questions.

### Table 2.3.A — Statistical Process Control (SPC) Formulas

| Parameter | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Mean (μ̂)** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Average of sample measurements |
| **Sample Standard Deviation (s)** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Spread within a subgroup |
| **Overall Std Dev (σ̂)** | $\bar{R}/d_2$ or $\bar{s}/c_4$ | Long-term process variation |
| **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** | $\bar{x} + 3\sigma$ | For X̄ chart (3-sigma limits) |
| **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** | $\bar{x} - 3\sigma$ | For X̄ chart |
| **USL / LSL** | Customer specification | Upper / Lower Specification Limit |
| **Process Capability (Cp)** | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability |
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $\min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Actual (centered) capability |
| **Pp / Ppk** | Same as Cp/Cpk using **overall σ** | Long-term performance |
| **DPMO (Six Sigma)** | $\dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities |
| **Yield (Y)** | $1 - \text{DPMO}/10^6$ | First-pass yield (rolled throughput) |
| **RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)** | $Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \dots \times Y_n$ | Yield across multiple process steps |

### Table 2.3.B — Cost of Quality (CoQ) Formulas

| Cost Type | Formula / Components |
|---|---|
| **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** | Prevention Cost + Appraisal Cost |
| **Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)** | Internal Failure + External Failure |
| **Total Cost of Quality (TCoQ)** | CoC + CoNC |
| **Optimum Quality Cost** | Derivative: $\dfrac{d(TCoQ)}{d(\text{Quality Level})} = 0$ |

### Table 2.3.C — Six Sigma Sigma Level Mapping

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.15 |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32 |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.379 |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.9767 |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966** |

---

## 2.4 Seven Tools of Quality (Ishikawa's Classic 7 QC Tools)

These are **mandatory** for KTU 2-mark / short-answer questions:

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone / Ishikawa)** — Root cause analysis
2. **Check Sheet** — Data collection structured by category
3. **Control Chart** — Monitor process variation over time
4. **Histogram** — Frequency distribution visualization
5. **Pareto Chart** — 80/20 rule prioritization
6. **Scatter Diagram** — Correlation between two variables
7. **Flowchart** — Process mapping

### Seven New (Management & Planning) Tools

1. Affinity Diagram, 2. Interrelationship Diagram, 3. Tree Diagram,
4. Matrix Diagram, 5. Matrix Data Analysis, 6. Arrow Diagram (PERT/CPM),
7. Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Tool / Concept | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **TQM** | Toyota Production System, Boeing Quality System |
| **Six Sigma** | Motorola (1986), GE, Honeywell — $10B+ saved |
| **5S** | Japanese factories, hospital operating rooms, airline cabins |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Automotive safety interlocks, USB connectors, SIM trays |
| **Kaizen** | Daily improvement boards, Gemba walks |
| **Baldrige Model** | Healthcare, education, manufacturing in USA |
| **EFQM** | European healthcare & public sector |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Process Capability Index (Cpk)

The process capability indices are **derived** from the relationship between **process variation** (σ) and **tolerance spread** (USL − LSL).

### Step 1: Define the Tolerance Window
The total allowable variation is the difference between the Upper Specification Limit and Lower Specification Limit:

$$
USL - LSL = \text{Tolerance Band}
$$

### Step 2: Define the Process Voice
The process voice (actual variation) is six times the standard deviation, covering 99.73% of measurements under a normal distribution:

$$
\text{Process Voice} = 6\sigma
$$

### Step 3: Formulate Cp (Potential Capability)
Cp is the ratio of tolerance band to process voice:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

**Interpretation:** If Cp ≥ 1.33, the process is *capable*. If Cp < 1.0, the process is *incapable*.

### Step 4: Account for Process Centering (Actual Capability — Cpk)
A process with Cp = 1.5 may still produce defects if it is *not centered* on the nominal value. We split the tolerance into two one-sided indices:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(C_{pu}, C_{pl}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Derive the One-Sided Indices

$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \quad C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

### Step 6: Combined Expression

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world meaning:** Cpk = 1.33 ⇒ Process is centered AND capable (3.4 defects per 1000). Cpk = 2.0 ⇒ Six Sigma level.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Six Sigma DPMO and Sigma Level Conversion

### Step 1: Define DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects Detected} \times 10^6}{\text{Number of Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}
$$

### Step 2: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A factory produces **5,000 units/day** of a printed circuit board. Each board has **100 soldering points** (opportunities). In a week (5 days), **127 solder defects** are reported. Calculate DPMO.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 5000 \times 100 = 500{,}000\ \text{per day}
$$

$$
\text{Weekly Opportunities} = 500{,}000 \times 5 = 2{,}500{,}000
$$

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{127 \times 10^6}{2{,}500{,}000} = 50.8
$$

### Step 3: Convert DPMO to Yield

$$
\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^6} = 1 - \dfrac{50.8}{10^6} = 0.9999492 = 99.9949\%
$$

### Step 4: Determine Sigma Level (using yield-to-sigma table)

For Yield ≈ 99.995%, the corresponding **sigma level ≈ 4.5σ**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost of Quality (CoQ) Mathematical Model

The **CoQ curve** shows the relationship between quality level and total cost.

### Step 1: Define Cost Functions

Let *q* = quality level (0% → 100% defect-free).

**Prevention & Appraisal Costs (CoC):** Increase as quality level rises.

$$
C_{CoC}(q) = a \cdot q^2 + b \cdot q
$$

where *a* and *b* are empirical constants.

**Failure Costs (CoNC):** Decrease as quality level rises.

$$
C_{CoNC}(q) = c \cdot (1-q)^2 + d \cdot (1-q)
$$

### Step 2: Total Cost of Quality

$$
TCoQ(q) = C_{CoC}(q) + C_{CoNC}(q)
$$

### Step 3: Find the Optimum (KTU 7-mark derivation)

Set derivative to zero:

$$
\dfrac{d(TCoQ)}{dq} = 2a \cdot q + b - 2c \cdot (1-q) - d = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
2a \cdot q + 2c \cdot q = 2c + d - b
$$

$$
q_{opt} = \dfrac{2c + d - b}{2(a+c)}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example

Given a = 1000, b = 500, c = 800, d = 300:

$$
q_{opt} = \dfrac{2(800) + 300 - 500}{2(1000 + 800)} = \dfrac{1600 + 300 - 500}{3600} = \dfrac{1400}{3600} = 0.3889
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{opt} \approx 38.89\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The optimum is typically around **80–85%** in real industries — meaning *very low defect levels may not be economically optimal*. This is the foundation of the "right-quality" philosophy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

For a multi-step process, defect multiplication reduces yield dramatically.

### Step 1: Individual Step Yields

$$
Y_i = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Defects at step }i}{\text{Input to step }i}
$$

### Step 2: RTY Formula

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example

A circuit board assembly has 4 steps with yields 99%, 98%, 99.5%, 99.7%:

$$
RTY = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.995 \times 0.997 = 0.9625
$$

This means **only 96.25%** of raw inputs emerge as defect-free outputs — even though every individual step exceeds 98%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Hidden Factory Effect:** A 99% yield per step across 10 steps yields only ~90% RTY. This is why TQM insists on *every* station achieving near-zero defects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TQM Implementation Roadmap (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Needs and Expectations] --> B[Top Management Commitment]
    B --> C[Strategic Quality Planning]
    C --> D[Process Mapping and SOP Design]
    D --> E[Employee Training and Empowerment]
    E --> F[PDCA Cycle Execution]
    F --> G[Statistical Process Control SPC]
    G --> H[Kaizen Continuous Improvement]
    H --> I[Customer Satisfaction Measurement]
    I --> J[Feedback Loop to Top Management]
    J --> B
```

---

## 4.2 Manufacturing Excellence Pillars (Baldrige + EFQM Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Leadership["LEADERSHIP and STRATEGY"]
        L1[Vision Mission Values]
        L2[Strategic Planning]
        L3[Governance]
    end

    subgraph Customers["CUSTOMER FOCUS"]
        C1[Voice of Customer VOC]
        C2[Customer Engagement]
        C3[Customer Satisfaction KPIs]
    end

    subgraph Operations["OPERATIONS MEASUREMENT ANALYSIS"]
        O1[Process Management]
        O2[Lean Six Sigma]
        O3[SPC and Digital Quality]
        O4[Supply Chain Quality]
    end

    subgraph Workforce["WORKFORCE ENGAGEMENT"]
        W1[Training and Competency]
        W2[Empowerment and Recognition]
        W3[Safety and Well Being]
    end

    subgraph Results["RESULTS DOMAIN"]
        R1[Product and Process Outcomes]
        R2[Customer Outcomes]
        R3[Workforce Outcomes]
        R4[Leadership and Financial Outcomes]
    end

    Leadership --> Operations
    Customers --> Operations
    Workforce --> Operations
    Operations --> Results
    Results --> Leadership
```

---

## 4.3 Six Sigma DMAIC Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[D Define Problem and VOC] --> B[M Measure Process Performance]
    B --> C[A Analyze Root Cause]
    C --> D[I Improve Solution Implementation]
    D --> E[C Control Standardization]
    E --> F{Goal Achieved?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Document Best Practice and Close Project]
    F -- No --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Kaizen Continuous Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plan Identify Problem] --> B[Do Implement Small Change]
    B --> C[Check Measure Outcome]
    C --> D[Act Standardize or Adjust]
    D --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Kaizen** = "Change for the Better" in Japanese. The cycle is intentionally small-step, daily, and employee-driven — a hallmark of Japanese manufacturing excellence.

---

## 4.5 5S Workplace Organization Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1S Seiri Sort] --> B[2S Seiton Set in Order]
    B --> C[3S Seiso Shine]
    C --> D[4S Seiketsu Standardize]
    D --> E[5S Shitsuke Sustain]
    E --> F[Visual Workplace and Zero Defects]
```

| 5S Term | Japanese | English | KTU Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1S | Seiri | Sort | Keep only essential items; remove the rest |
| 2S | Seiton | Set in Order | A place for everything, everything in its place |
| 3S | Seiso | Shine | Clean the workplace and identify abnormalities |
| 4S | Seiketsu | Standardize | Establish norms and visual standards |
| 5S | Shitsuke | Sustain | Maintain discipline and continuously improve |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Total Quality Management. List any four principles of TQM. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Total Quality Management is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that aims to embed quality in every functional area, process, and employee activity to deliver superior value to customers through continuous improvement and prevention-based defect elimination.

**Four Principles (0.375 marks each):**
1. **Customer Focus** — Understanding and exceeding customer expectations
2. **Leadership** — Establishing unity of purpose and direction
3. **Engagement of People** — Competent, empowered people at all levels
4. **Process Approach** — Managing activities as interrelated processes

---

### Question 2: What is Six Sigma? Explain its significance in manufacturing. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology and statistical quality improvement approach that seeks to reduce defects to **3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**, achieving near-zero variability.

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. Improves profitability by minimizing rework, scrap, and warranty costs
2. Enhances customer satisfaction and brand reputation
3. Provides a structured **DMAIC framework** for problem-solving
4. Aligns quality improvement with strategic business goals

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Deming Wheel (PDCA Cycle)** and its role in TQM implementation. Discuss how it drives continuous improvement with a real-time industrial example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC machining process produces **axle shafts** with specification **LSL = 24.95 mm**, **USL = 25.05 mm**. The process mean is **μ = 25.03 mm** and standard deviation is **σ = 0.012 mm**. Compute **Cp** and **Cpk**, and comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a) — Deming Wheel (PDCA)

**Deming Wheel (PDCA) (3 marks):**

The **PDCA cycle** is a four-stage iterative management method developed by **W. Edwards Deming**:

$$
\boxed{\text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act}}
$$

| Stage | Action | Industrial Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Identify the problem, analyze the process, define objectives | Kaizen team identifies high rejection rate in axle machining |
| **Do** | Implement the planned action on a small scale | New cutting tool installed on one machine; trial 50 shafts |
| **Check** | Measure results against objectives | Rejection dropped from 8% to 2.5% on the trial machine |
| **Act** | Standardize and roll out, OR refine and restart | SOP updated; tool change interval reduced from 200 to 150 parts; rolled out plant-wide |

**Role in Continuous Improvement (4 marks):**
- The cycle is **never-ending** — every solved problem uncovers the next improvement opportunity
- Provides a **scientific, data-based** approach — replacing guesswork with measurement
- **Empowers shop-floor workers** to run micro-experiments
- Integrates seamlessly with **TQM, ISO 9001, and Six Sigma DMAIC**
- Real example: **Toyota Production System** runs over **10,000 PDCA cycles per year per plant**, accumulating tens of thousands of small improvements

---

#### Solution A(b) — Process Capability Calculation

**Given:**
- LSL = 24.95 mm, USL = 25.05 mm
- μ = 25.03 mm, σ = 0.012 mm

**Step 1: Compute Cp (2 marks)**

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.072}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_p = 1.389}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Cpu (2 marks)**

$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.05 - 25.03}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.036}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pu} = 0.556}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Cpl (2 marks)**

$$
C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.03 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.036}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pl} = 2.222}
$$

**Step 4: Cpk and Interpretation (1 mark)**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(0.556, 2.222) = \boxed{0.556}
$$

**Comment (Bonus for full marks):**
- Cp = 1.389 indicates *capable* potential (above 1.33 threshold)
- Cpk = 0.556 is **far below 1.33** → Process is **not centered**; it is drifting toward the USL
- **Action:** Re-center the machine (e.g., shift μ from 25.03 to 25.00 mm) to achieve Cpk > 1.33

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Cost of Quality (CoQ) concept**. With a neat diagram, discuss the four categories of quality costs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company producing mobile phone casings has recorded the following monthly quality costs: Prevention = ₹80,000, Appraisal = ₹50,000, Internal Failure = ₹1,20,000, External Failure = ₹2,50,000. Calculate (i) Cost of Conformance, (ii) Cost of Non-Conformance, and (iii) Total Cost of Quality. Suggest strategies to reduce the total CoQ. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a) — Cost of Quality (CoQ)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Cost of Quality (CoQ) is the sum of all costs incurred to ensure good quality (prevention + appraisal) plus all costs incurred due to poor quality (internal + external failure).

**Four Categories (4 marks):**

| Category | Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs** | Conformance | Training, quality planning, SOP development, supplier capability audits |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Conformance | Inspection, testing, calibration of gauges, QC audits |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Non-Conformance | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime, material wastage |
| **External Failure Costs** | Non-Conformance | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, customer complaints, legal liability |

**Diagrammatic Explanation (1 mark — verbal diagram):**

A typical CoQ curve shows:
- Prevention + Appraisal costs **rise** as quality level improves
- Internal + External failure costs **fall** as quality level improves
- Total CoQ reaches a **minimum at an optimum quality level (~85–95%)**, then rises again

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry insight:** Most factories spend **70% of their quality budget on failure costs** and only **30% on prevention**. World-class organizations invert this to **60% prevention / 40% failure**.

---

#### Solution B(b) — CoQ Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**

| Cost Type | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Prevention | 80,000 |
| Appraisal | 50,000 |
| Internal Failure | 1,20,000 |
| External Failure | 2,50,000 |

**(i) Cost of Conformance (CoC) (2 marks):**

$$
C_{CoC} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} = 80{,}000 + 50{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{CoC} = \text{₹ } 1{,}30{,}000}
$$

**(ii) Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC) (2 marks):**

$$
C_{CoNC} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{CoNC} = \text{₹ } 3{,}70{,}000}
$$

**(iii) Total Cost of Quality (2 marks):**

$$
TCoQ = 1{,}30{,}000 + 3{,}70{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{TCoQ = \text{₹ } 5{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Cost Ratio Analysis (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Conformance Ratio} = \dfrac{1{,}30{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 26\%
$$

$$
\text{Non-Conformance Ratio} = \dfrac{3{,}70{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 74\%
$$

**Strategies to Reduce TCoQ (Bonus for full marks):**

1. **Increase Prevention spending** — invest more in operator training, robust design (DFMEA), supplier audits
2. **Reduce External Failure** — implement Poka-Yoke at assembly stations, strengthen in-process inspection
3. **Shift quality upstream** — adopt **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** to eliminate defects at the design stage
4. **Implement SPC** — replace 100% inspection with statistical sampling and control charts
5. **Vendor Development** — certify suppliers to **IATF 16949 / ISO 9001** to reduce incoming defect rates
6. **Lean initiatives** — 5S, Value Stream Mapping, mistake-proofing to eliminate rework

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on TQM Questions**
> - **Confusing Cp and Cpk:** Cp = potential capability (assumes centered); Cpk = actual (accounts for shift). Both are required in numericals.
> - **Mixing "Prevention" with "Appraisal":** Prevention avoids defects *before* they occur; Appraisal *detects* defects after they occur.
> - **Ignoring RTY:** Students often quote single-step yields without computing the rolled throughput.
> - **PDCA ≠ DMAIC:** PDCA is general continuous improvement; DMAIC is Six Sigma-specific. Do NOT interchange.
> - **TQM ≠ ISO 9000:** TQM is a *philosophy*; ISO 9000 is a *certifiable standard* aligned with TQM principles.
> - **Always show units** in numericals (mm, %, DPMO). Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction in valuation.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **TQM** = Organization-wide quality philosophy centered on customer satisfaction and continuous improvement
- ✅ **Eight ISO 9000:2015 Principles** = Customer Focus, Leadership, People Engagement, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decisions, Relationship Management, Systematic Management
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle (Deming)** = Plan → Do → Check → Act (iterative, never-ending)
- ✅ **Cost of Quality** = Prevention + Appraisal (Conformance) + Internal Failure + External Failure (Non-Conformance)
- ✅ **Cp ≥ 1.33** = Capable process; **Cpk ≥ 1.33** = Capable AND centered
- ✅ **Six Sigma** = 3.4 DPMO ≈ 99.99966% yield
- ✅ **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control
- ✅ **5S** = Seiri (Sort), Seiton (Set), Seiso (Shine), Seiketsu (Standardize), Shitsuke (Sustain)
- ✅ **7 QC Tools** = Fishbone, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** = Mistake-proofing device (Toyota concept)
- ✅ **Kaizen** = Continuous, incremental, employee-driven improvement
- ✅ **Jidoka** = Autonomation — stop the line when abnormality occurs
- ✅ **Just-In-Time (JIT)** = Produce only what is needed, when needed, in the amount needed
- ✅ **Baldrige / EFQM** = Excellence models used to benchmark Manufacturing Excellence
- ✅ **Quality Gurus**: Deming (PDCA), Juran (Quality Trilogy), Crosby (Zero Defects), Ishikawa (Fishbone), Taguchi (Loss Function), Feigenbaum (Total Quality Control), Imai (Kaizen)
- ✅ **RTY** = product of individual step yields — always lower than any single-step yield
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 + TQM** = Smart sensors, digital twins, AI-driven predictive quality, real-time SPC dashboards

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Preparation Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(i) Definition (2 marks) → (ii) Concept Explanation with diagram (3 marks) → (iii) Numerical/Application (2 marks)**. Examiners reward **neat block diagrams, correct formulas, and final numerical values with units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deming's Approach to Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT866 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deming's Approach to Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**W. Edwards Deming's Approach to Quality** is a holistic management philosophy that defines quality not as a product attribute, but as a **predictable degree of uniformity and dependability** at the lowest possible cost, conforming to a defined specification. For Deming, quality is fundamentally a **management responsibility**—rooted in systemic improvement, statistical control, and continuous learning—rather than the sole duty of the shop-floor worker or the quality inspection department.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the *Total Quality Management (TQM)* framework:

> **Quality (Deming's Definition):** *"The capacity of a system to deliver products and services that satisfy customer needs, achieved through the statistical reduction of variation in processes, the elimination of waste, and the cultivation of a culture of continuous improvement."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT866 Module 3):**
> Deming's approach is one of the **four master quality philosophies** (alongside Juran, Crosby, and Ishikawa). Examiners frequently test his **14 Points**, the **PDSA Cycle (Deming Wheel)**, the **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)**, and the **Chain Reaction** model.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **train conductor** running a passenger train between two cities. The train must arrive at the destination at a precise scheduled time—say, **10:00 AM sharp**, not "around 10 AM."

- If the train arrives at **9:15 AM**, the platform is empty, and passengers miss it.
- If it arrives at **10:45 AM**, passengers have already left the platform in frustration.
- Only when it arrives in a **narrow, predictable window (e.g., 9:58 AM to 10:02 AM)** does the system work reliably for everyone.

**Deming's approach to quality is exactly this idea applied to manufacturing and services.** It does not matter that your average arrival time is "10:00 AM" if some days you are 15 minutes early and other days 45 minutes late. What matters is **reducing the variation** around the target so that outcomes become **uniform and predictable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight (Deming's 85/15 Rule):**
> Deming famously stated that **85% of quality problems** are caused by **poor management and flawed systems**, while only **15%** are attributable to the worker. This single idea revolutionized Japanese industry after WWII and forms the bedrock of KTU Module 3.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Board (KTU High-Frequency Definitions)

| Term | Meaning (Exam-Ready) |
|---|---|
| **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** | Use of control charts to monitor process variation. |
| **Common Cause Variation** | Inherent, systemic variation built into the process. |
| **Special Cause Variation** | External, assignable variation due to specific events. |
| **PDSA Cycle** | Plan–Do–Study–Act iterative improvement loop. |
| **System of Profound Knowledge** | Four interrelated knowledge domains: Appreciation, Theory, Psychology, Knowledge of Variation. |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous incremental improvement (Deming-influenced). |
| **Chain Reaction** | Improve quality → lower costs → capture market → stay in business. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deming's 14 Points for Management (Board-Exam Gold)

Deming condensed his philosophy into **14 Obligations of Top Management**. KTU examiners expect at minimum a 7-mark answer covering this. Each point is an **obligation of leadership**, not a suggestion.

### **Pillar 1: Constancy of Purpose**
- **Point 1:** Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service. Aim to become competitive, stay in business, and provide jobs through innovation, research, and continual improvement.

### **Pillar 2: The New Philosophy**
- **Point 2:** Adopt the new philosophy. We are in a new economic age. Western management must awaken to the challenge, learn their responsibilities, and take on leadership for change.

### **Pillar 3: Inspection is Not the Answer**
- **Point 3:** Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality. Eliminate the need for mass inspection by building quality into the product in the first place. *(End the "inspect quality in" mindset.)*

### **Pillar 4: Stop Awarding Business on Price Alone**
- **Point 4:** End the practice of awarding business on the basis of price tag. Instead, minimize total cost. Move toward a single supplier for any one item, on a long-term relationship of loyalty and trust.

### **Pillar 5: Improve Every Process**
- **Point 5:** Improve constantly and forever the system of production and service to improve quality and productivity, and thus constantly decrease costs.

### **Pillar 6: Institute Training on the Job**
- **Point 6:** Institute training on the job for everyone, including management, to enable people to do their jobs better.

### **Pillar 7: Institute Leadership**
- **Point 7:** Institute leadership. The aim of supervision should be to help people and machines and gadgets to do a better job. Supervision of management is in need of overhaul, as well as supervision of production workers.

### **Pillar 8: Drive Out Fear**
- **Point 8:** Drive out fear, so that everyone may work effectively for the company. No one can put in their best performance unless they feel secure.

### **Pillar 9: Break Down Barriers**
- **Point 9:** Break down barriers between departments. People in research, design, sales, and production must work as a team to foresee problems of production and use that may be encountered with the product or service.

### **Pillar 10: Eliminate Slogans and Exhortations**
- **Point 10:** Eliminate slogans and exhortations for the workforce which ask for zero defects and new levels of productivity without providing methods to achieve them.

### **Pillar 11: Eliminate Numerical Quotas**
- **Point 11:** Eliminate numerical quotas for the workforce and numerical goals for management. Substitute leadership.

### **Pillar 12: Remove Barriers to Pride of Workmanship**
- **Point 12:** Remove barriers that rob people of their right to pride of workmanship. The annual rating of merit must go.

### **Pillar 13: Institute a Vigorous Programme of Education & Self-Improvement**
- **Point 13:** Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement for everyone.

### **Pillar 14: Put Everybody to Work to Accomplish the Transformation**
- **Point 14:** Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation. The transformation is everybody's job.

## 2.2 The Deming Chain Reaction (Mathematical Foundation)

The **Chain Reaction** is a sequence of cause-and-effect events that justifies quality investment in financial terms. It is highly testable because it contains a **mathematical cost model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's Chain Reaction Sequence:**
> **Improve Quality → Costs Decrease (due to fewer rework, scrap, warranty) → Productivity Increases → Prices Can Be Lower → Market Share Expands → Business Stays in Business → Provides Jobs → Returns on Investment Increase.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Exam Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deming's Cost Reduction Model** | $\Delta C = (R_c + S_c + W_c) \cdot N_{def}$ | $\Delta C$ = cost saved; $R_c$ = rework cost/unit; $S_c$ = scrap cost/unit; $W_c$ = warranty cost/unit; $N_{def}$ = number of defects avoided | Use to defend ROI of quality |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL$ = upper spec limit; $LSL$ = lower spec limit; $\sigma$ = standard deviation | Deming's basis for "predictable variation" |
| **Six Sigma Quality Level** | $\text{DPMO} = 3.4$ per million opportunities | $\text{DPMO}$ = Defects Per Million Opportunities | Linked to $\pm 6\sigma$ from mean |
| **85/15 Rule (Deming's Attribution)** | $\text{System Fault} = 0.85 \cdot Q_{prob}$; $\text{Worker Fault} = 0.15 \cdot Q_{prob}$ | $Q_{prob}$ = total quality problems | Often asked as MCQ/short answer |
| **PDSA Iterative Improvement** | $K_{n+1} = K_n + \alpha \cdot \Delta P_n$ | $K_n$ = current knowledge/performance; $\alpha$ = learning rate; $\Delta P_n$ = study-phase improvement delta | Conceptual iterative model |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}$ | All costs in monetary units (₹, $, €) | Foundation of "lower costs" claim |
| **Supplier Quality Index** | $SQI = \dfrac{N_{accepted}}{N_{inspected}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{accepted}$ = accepted lots; $N_{inspected}$ = inspected lots | Point 4 justification |
| **Reduction in Variation** | $\sigma_{new} = \sigma_{old} \cdot (1 - r)$ | $r$ = reduction ratio ($0 \le r \le 1$) | Used in capability improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Pipe Safety:** In all the above formulas, the absolute value or set-membership bar uses `\vert` notation only when isolated; within tables the divide symbol $\div$ is replaced with the fraction slash to keep markdown tables intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Toyota Production System (TPS):** Built directly on Deming's teachings via JUSE (Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers).
- **Six Sigma (Motorola, 1986):** $\pm 6\sigma$ specification traces back to Deming's call for **"uniformity at the lowest cost."**
- **ISO 9001 / IATF 16949:** Process approach and risk-based thinking are Deming's PDCA in modern certification form.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** *Muda* (waste) and *Kaizen* (continuous improvement) originate in Deming's philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Deming's Cost-of-Quality Trade-Off Function

To prove mathematically that **higher quality reduces total cost**, we derive the relationship between quality investment and total cost of poor quality.

### **Step 1: Define Total Quality Cost Function**

Let total quality-related cost $TQC$ be the sum of prevention, appraisal, and failure costs:

$$TQC = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}$$

### **Step 2: Express Failure Costs in Terms of Defect Rate**

Let $D$ be the defect rate (defects per unit). As $D$ increases, internal and external failure costs rise **linearly with a steep slope**, because each defect triggers rework, scrap, or warranty claims. We model this as:

$$C_{internal\ failure} = k_i \cdot D$$

$$C_{external\ failure} = k_e \cdot D$$

where $k_i$ and $k_e$ are the internal and external cost coefficients (currency per defect).

### **Step 3: Express Prevention Cost**

Prevention cost is a function of the **level of quality investment** $Q$ (e.g., training, process redesign). Empirically, prevention grows with diminishing returns. A simple parabolic model:

$$C_{prevention} = a \cdot Q^2$$

where $a > 0$ captures the cost of building quality infrastructure.

### **Step 4: Relate Defect Rate to Quality Investment**

Higher $Q$ lowers the defect rate, typically via an exponential decay relationship:

$$D(Q) = D_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda Q}$$

where $D_0$ is the initial defect rate (at $Q=0$) and $\lambda$ is the **learning/investment efficiency coefficient**.

### **Step 5: Substitute into TQC**

$$TQC(Q) = a Q^2 + C_{appraisal} + (k_i + k_e) \cdot D_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda Q}$$

For minimization, we treat $C_{appraisal}$ as a constant baseline $C_a$ and take the derivative with respect to $Q$:

$$\frac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 2aQ - \lambda(k_i + k_e) D_0 e^{-\lambda Q} = 0$$

### **Step 6: Solve for Optimal $Q^*$**

Rearranging:

$$2aQ^* = \lambda(k_i + k_e) D_0 e^{-\lambda Q^*}$$

$$Q^* e^{\lambda Q^*} = \frac{\lambda(k_i + k_e) D_0}{2a}$$

This transcendental form has a solution in terms of the **Lambert W function** $W(\cdot)$:

$$Q^* = \frac{1}{\lambda} W\!\left(\frac{\lambda(k_i + k_e) D_0}{2a}\right)$$

### **Step 7: Interpretation**

This derivation proves Deming's intuitive claim with mathematical rigour:

- **$Q = 0$ (no prevention):** $TQC$ is dominated by the failure costs $(k_i+k_e)D_0$, which is **enormous**.
- **$Q \to \infty$ (over-investment):** $aQ^2$ dominates, $TQC$ rises again quadratically.
- **$Q = Q^*$:** $TQC$ is **minimized** at a finite optimal investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Deming's "Improve quality → costs decrease" is valid in the **low-$Q$ region** (where most companies operate). As you cross $Q^*$, marginal cost of prevention exceeds marginal savings. This is why Deming emphasises **systemic, incremental** improvement rather than maximalist spending.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Process Capability and the Six Sigma Bridge

### **Step 1: Define Specification Width**

$$W = USL - LSL$$

For a two-sided specification, this is the allowable variation range.

### **Step 2: Define Natural Process Tolerance**

For a stable process, the **natural tolerance** is the $\pm 3\sigma$ spread (covers **99.73%** of output under normality):

$$\text{Natural Tolerance} = 6\sigma$$

### **Step 3: Compute Capability Index**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{W}{6\sigma}$$

### **Step 4: Interpretation Table**

| $C_p$ value | Process Capability | Deming's Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| $C_p < 1.0$ | Process spread exceeds spec | **Incapable** — cannot meet design intent |
| $1.0 \le C_p < 1.33$ | Marginal capability | **Barely acceptable**, monitor closely |
| $1.33 \le C_p < 1.67$ | Good capability | **Industry standard** target |
| $C_p \ge 1.67$ | Excellent capability | **Six Sigma** zone ($4.5\sigma$ shifted) |
| $C_p = 2.0$ | World-class | Six Sigma ($\pm 6\sigma$ from mean = **3.4 DPMO**) |

### **Step 5: Translate to DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**

For a process centered between LSL and USL:

$$Z_{LSL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}, \quad Z_{USL} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Total defect rate in parts per million:

$$\text{DPMO} = \left[\Phi(-Z_{LSL}) + \Phi(-Z_{USL})\right] \times 10^6$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

For $Z_{USL} = -Z_{LSL} = 6$ (perfectly centered, $6\sigma$ each side):

$$\text{DPMO} = 2 \times \Phi(-6) \times 10^6 \approx 2 \times (0.9999999999\ldots) \times 10^6 \approx 3.4$$

This is the famous **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO** result.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Likely Question Pattern:** "Explain how Deming's concept of variation reduction leads to the Six Sigma framework." This derivation is your answer.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Deming's Approach

| Situation | Deming's Recommended Action | Justification (Point #) |
|---|---|---|
| High defect rate across plant | Improve process, not worker | Points 3, 7, 11 |
| Workers afraid of reporting errors | Drive out fear | Point 8 |
| Departments blaming each other | Cross-functional teamwork | Point 9 |
| Customer complaints rising | Reduce variation in design | Points 1, 5 |
| Quality slogans like "Zero Defects" posted everywhere | Remove them; provide methods | Point 10 |
| Annual performance ratings killing morale | Eliminate merit ratings | Point 12 |
| Buyers switching suppliers every quarter | Single supplier, long-term trust | Point 4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define quality according to W. Edwards Deming. How is it different from the conventional "inspection-based" definition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Deming defined quality as **"a predictable degree of uniformity and dependability at low cost, suited to the market."** Unlike the conventional view—which treated quality as a post-production attribute verified by **inspection** (sorting good from bad)—Deming argued that quality must be **built into the process**. He stated that **85% of quality problems are systemic (management responsibility)** and only **15% are attributable to workers**. Hence, reliance on mass inspection is obsolete (Point 3); quality comes from process capability and statistical control.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. List Deming's 14 Points in any three logical categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Strategic:** Constancy of purpose (Pt 1), New philosophy (Pt 2).
2. **Process & Supplier:** Build quality in (Pt 3), Single supplier (Pt 4), Continuous improvement (Pt 5).
3. **People:** Training (Pt 6), Leadership (Pt 7), Drive out fear (Pt 8), Pride of workmanship (Pt 12).
4. **System Removal:** Break silos (Pt 9), No slogans (Pt 10), No quotas (Pt 11).
5. **Transformation:** Education (Pt 13), All work for transformation (Pt 14).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain Deming's **14 Points for Management** in detail. Categorize them into purpose, system, variation, psychology, and theory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain Deming's **PDSA Cycle** and describe how it drives continuous improvement in a manufacturing firm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Deming's 14 Points are 14 obligations placed on **top management** to bring about a transformation in organizational culture, quality, and productivity. They are not sequential choices; they constitute a **system of interdependent principles**.

**Categorization (6 marks):**

**Category 1 — Purpose & Philosophy (Points 1, 2, 14):**
- Point 1 demands **constancy of purpose** — innovation, research, long-term planning instead of short-term profit.
- Point 2 calls for adopting the **new philosophy** — recognizing that the economic environment has changed.
- Point 14 makes transformation **everyone's job**.

**Category 2 — System & Process (Points 3, 4, 5, 9):**
- Point 3: Eliminate mass inspection; **build quality in**.
- Point 4: End lowest-price purchasing; use **single supplier** on long-term trust.
- Point 5: Continuously improve production and service systems.
- Point 9: Break **barriers between departments**.

**Category 3 — Variation (Point 3, implicit in 5, 7):**
- Reduce variation through **process capability**, statistical control, leadership over blame.

**Category 4 — Psychology (Points 8, 10, 11, 12):**
- Point 8: **Drive out fear** — fear suppresses learning.
- Point 10: Eliminate **slogans** without methods.
- Point 11: Eliminate **numerical quotas**.
- Point 12: Restore **pride of workmanship**, abolish annual merit ratings.

**Category 5 — Knowledge & Learning (Points 6, 7, 13):**
- Point 6: Institute **on-the-job training**.
- Point 7: **Leadership** — supervision that helps, not coerces.
- Point 13: Education and self-improvement for all.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list all 14 points without categorization. KTU rewards **structured grouping** with 2 extra marks. Avoid writing them as a flat list.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**PDSA Cycle Definition (1 mark):**
The **Plan–Do–Study–Act (PDSA) cycle**, also called the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart cycle**, is an iterative four-step scientific method for continuous improvement.

**Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks):**

| Stage | Activity | Tools / Output |
|---|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Identify a problem or opportunity; analyze current process; hypothesize an improvement; design the change. | Process map, fishbone diagram, hypothesis statement |
| **DO** | Implement the change on a **small scale** (pilot run); collect data rigorously. | Trial data, observations, deviations logged |
| **STUDY** | Compare the **actual results** with the **predicted results** from the PLAN stage. Use SPC, control charts, hypothesis tests. | Statistical analysis, learning, revised knowledge |
| **ACT** | If the change works → **standardize** it across the plant, update SOPs. If not → revise the hypothesis and loop back to PLAN. | New standard operating procedure, OR next iteration |

**Application in a Manufacturing Firm (1 mark):**
Consider a CNC machining cell producing a shaft with a diameter specification of $30 \pm 0.05$ mm. Defect rate is **8%**.
- **PLAN:** Hypothesis — adjusting coolant flow will reduce thermal distortion and bring $C_p$ above 1.33.
- **DO:** Pilot run with new coolant flow on 50 shafts.
- **STUDY:** $C_p$ rose from 1.10 to 1.45; defect rate dropped to 1.5%.
- **ACT:** Standardize the new coolant flow across all 12 CNC cells; train operators.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** with **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act)**. KTU Module 3 strictly uses **PDSA** for Deming. Writing "Check" instead of "Study" will lose 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain **Deming's Chain Reaction** with a mathematical cost model. Show how improved quality leads to lower total cost and higher market share. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Deming's **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)**. How does it differ from traditional quality assurance models? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Chain Reaction Introduction (1 mark):**
Deming's Chain Reaction establishes the **business case for quality**: improving quality is not a cost burden—it is a **profit driver** through systemic cost reduction.

**Sequence (2 marks):**
$$\text{Quality} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Rework, scrap, warranty} \downarrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Productivity} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Unit cost} \downarrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Price} \downarrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Market share} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Survival} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Employment} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{ROI} \uparrow$$

**Mathematical Model (3 marks):**

Let $N$ = number of units produced per year.
Let $C_r$, $C_s$, $C_w$ = per-unit cost of rework, scrap, and warranty respectively.
Let $d$ = defect rate (fraction).

Annual quality-related cost:

$$C_{total}(d) = N \cdot d \cdot (C_r + C_s + C_w) + C_{prevention}(Q)$$

If improved quality reduces defect rate from $d_1$ to $d_2$ (with $d_2 < d_1$):

$$\Delta C_{savings} = N \cdot (d_1 - d_2) \cdot (C_r + C_s + C_w)$$

**Numerical Example (1 mark):**
Suppose $N = 100{,}000$ units/year, $d_1 = 0.08$, $d_2 = 0.02$, $C_r + C_s + C_w = ₹500$ per defect.

$$\Delta C_{savings} = 100{,}000 \times (0.08 - 0.02) \times 500 = ₹30{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

This ₹30 lakh saving justifies process improvement expenditure, validating the chain.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**SoPK Definition (1 mark):**
The **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** is Deming's integrated framework for understanding and improving systems, composed of **four interrelated components**.

**The Four Components (4 marks):**

1. **Appreciation for a System:** A system is a network of **interdependent components** working toward a common aim. Optimize the **whole**, not sub-parts. Every component must understand the system's aim.

2. **Knowledge of Variation:** Variation is **inherent** in every process. Distinguish:
   - **Common cause** (systemic) → reduce via process redesign.
   - **Special cause** (assignable) → eliminate via root-cause analysis.
   Use **control charts** to tell them apart.

3. **Theory of Knowledge (Epistemology):** Knowledge comes from **prediction, observation, and comparison**. Deming embraced the **scientific method**: theory → prediction → test → revise.

4. **Psychology:** People are different. **Fear destroys learning**. Leaders must understand intrinsic motivation, team dynamics, and the limits of human cognition.

**Differences from Traditional QA (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional QA | Deming's SoPK |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Inspect, sort, reject | Understand system, reduce variation |
| **Defects** | Worker fault | System fault (85%) |
| **Improvement** | Reactive (after defect) | Proactive (PDSA, continuous) |
| **Standards** | Fixed specification | Capability, statistical limits |
| **People** | Replaceable operators | Intrinsically motivated learners |
| **Customer** | External satisfaction | System-wide purpose alignment |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write SoPK as a list without explaining the **interdependence** of the four components. KTU expects you to state explicitly that "all four must be applied **simultaneously** for transformation."

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Mark Loss Areas

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Pitfalls in PESFT866 Module 3 Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing PDCA with PDSA.** Deming's cycle is **PDSA** (Study, not Check).
> 2. **Writing 14 Points as a flat list.** KTU demands **categorization**.
> 3. **Omitting the 85/15 rule.** Any Deming answer without this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the SoPK's four-pillar structure.** SoPK = Appreciation + Variation + Theory + Psychology — must be in **this order**.
> 5. **Forgetting mathematical justification.** Chain Reaction answers must include a **cost equation or numerical example** to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Quality (Deming):** *Predictable uniformity & dependability at low cost* — not inspection-based conformance.
- **Core Philosophy:** Quality is a **management responsibility**; 85% of problems are **systemic**, 15% are **worker-related**.
- **14 Points** are obligations on **top management**, not a checklist. They are **interdependent**.
- **PDSA Cycle** is **iterative** and **scientific**: Plan → Do → Study → Act. Note: **Study, not Check.**
- **System of Profound Knowledge** has **four pillars**: Appreciation for System, Knowledge of Variation, Theory of Knowledge, Psychology.
- **Common vs Special Cause Variation:** Common = system → redesign; Special = assignable → root cause.
- **Chain Reaction:** Quality ↑ → Cost ↓ → Productivity ↑ → Market Share ↑ → Survival ↑.
- **Capability Index $C_p$** = $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$; Six Sigma = $C_p = 2.0$ = **3.4 DPMO**.
- **Cost of Quality** has four buckets: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.
- **Supplier Management:** Deming advocated **single supplier** with **long-term trust** (Point 4).
- **Anti-patterns Deming rejected:** Slogans (Pt 10), Quotas (Pt 11), Annual merit ratings (Pt 12), Departmental silos (Pt 9), Mass inspection (Pt 3).
- **Japan connection:** Deming taught at JUSE in 1950; influenced Toyota, Sony, Canon, Honda, Komatsu.
- **Examiner's favourite 14-mark Q:** *14 Points + PDSA + Chain Reaction* (one full answer per choice).
- **Examiner's favourite 3-mark Q:** *Definition of Quality + 85/15 Rule + SoPK pillars.*
- **Mnemonic for 14 Points:** *"Constant purpose, New philosophy, Build quality, Single supplier, Improve constantly, Train, Lead, Drive fear out, Break silos, Remove slogans, No quotas, Pride of work, Education, Transform."*
- **One-line conclusion for any Deming answer:** *"Quality is not an act, it is a system — and that system is management's job."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
