# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define World Class Manufacturing. State any **four** critical success factors for achieving WCM. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** World Class Manufacturing (WCM) is a comprehensive management philosophy and integrated system of practices that enables a manufacturing enterprise to achieve and sustain best-in-class performance in quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and innovation — matching or exceeding the standards set by global industry leaders.
>
> **Four CSFs:**
> 1. **Top Management Leadership and Commitment** — providing vision, resources, and cultural modeling.
> 2. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — driving OEE to ≥85% through proactive equipment care.
> 3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen / TQM)** — incremental, employee-driven process enhancement.
> 4. **Just-in-Time (JIT) / Lean Manufacturing** — pull-based, waste-eliminating production flow.
>
> *(Other valid CSFs: Customer Focus, HR Empowerment, Technology & Supply Chain Integration.)*

### Q2. What is Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)? Write its formula and state the world-class benchmark. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** OEE is a composite metric that quantifies the effectiveness of a manufacturing equipment by combining three independent loss categories — *Availability*, *Performance*, and *Quality* — into a single percentage.
>
> **Formula:**
>
> $$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$
>
> where
> - $A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$
> - $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$
> - $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$
>
> **World-class benchmark:** OEE ≥ **85%** (as established by Seiichi Nakajima, TPM authority).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 — Listing without integrating:** Many students merely *list* the CSFs and earn 5–6 of 14 marks. KTU rewards students who draw **interrelationships** (e.g., "JIT cannot succeed without TPM" or "Kaizen needs HR empowerment"). Always end a CSF answer with a synthesis paragraph.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 — Confusing TPM with TQM:** TPM is *equipment-focused* (8 pillars, OEE); TQM is *process-quality-focused* (customer, defect reduction). Examiners deduct 2 marks for this conflation.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 — Skipping units in numerical answers:** In OEE/takt time problems, **always** write the unit (%, minutes, pieces/hr). Marks are deducted for unit omission in 2024 scheme scripts.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 — Not stating assumptions:** When computing OEE, state "Performance is capped at 100% per OEE convention" to demonstrate rigor.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the concept of Critical Success Factors (CSFs). Identify and discuss the **seven critical success factors** for achieving World Class Manufacturing in a modern enterprise. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** A bottling plant operates **two shifts of 8 hours each per day**, with **30 minutes of planned downtime per shift**. The plant experienced **90 minutes of unplanned breakdown** and **40 minutes of changeover** during the day. It produced **15,000 bottles** during the operating period. The ideal cycle time is **6 seconds per bottle** and quality inspection rejected **300 bottles**. Compute the OEE and interpret the result with respect to world-class standards. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A.(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
> CSFs are the limited set of areas in which satisfactory results are essential for an organization to achieve its mission. In WCM, CSFs represent the strategic and operational capabilities without which best-in-class performance is unattainable.

**The Seven CSFs (6 marks — ~1 mark each, with a brief explanation):**

1. **Top Management Leadership & Commitment** — Top management must provide visible, sustained commitment through capital allocation, gemba walks, and long-term horizon. WCM is a 5–10 year journey; without leadership, initiatives die.

2. **Customer Focus & Voice of Customer (VOC)** — Systematic capture of customer needs via QFD, NPS, complaint analysis. WCM firms treat customer satisfaction as the *primary* performance measure.

3. **Total Quality Management (TQM) & Continuous Improvement** — Kaizen, PDCA, Six Sigma, Poka-Yoke. TQM drives the *quality culture* and feeds improvements into all other CSFs.

4. **Just-in-Time (JIT) & Lean Manufacturing** — Pull-based flow, SMED, reduced WIP, takt-time alignment. JIT minimizes inventory and exposes problems for resolution.

5. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — The 8 pillars (autonomous, planned, quality maintenance, etc.) drive OEE to ≥85%. Without TPM, JIT is impossible because breakdowns halt the line.

6. **Human Resource Management & Empowerment** — Suggestion systems, quality circles, cross-training, gainsharing. WCM treats employees as *value creators*, not cost centers.

7. **Technology, Innovation & Supply Chain Integration** — CAD/CAM, MES, ERP, IoT, Industry 4.0, and long-term supplier partnerships. Digital and physical infrastructure enabling rapid, accurate, integrated operations.

**Synthesis (1 mark):**
> The seven CSFs are **not a checklist but an interlocked system**. CSFs 1 and 6 form the *socio-cultural foundation*; CSFs 3, 4, 5 form the *operational engine*; CSFs 2 and 7 form the *external/digital enablers*. World-class performance emerges only when **all seven are addressed simultaneously and iteratively**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A.(b) — OEE Numerical

**Given Data:**
- 2 shifts × 8 hours = 960 minutes/day
- Planned downtime: 30 min/shift × 2 = 60 minutes
- Unplanned breakdown: 90 minutes
- Changeover: 40 minutes
- N (total pieces) = 15,000 bottles
- ICT (ideal cycle time) = 6 seconds = 0.1 minutes
- Defective pieces D = 300 → G (good) = 15,000 − 300 = 14,700

**Step 1 — Planned Production Time (PPT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PPT} &= (2 \times 8 \times 60) - 60 \\
           &= 960 - 60 \\
           &= 900 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Operating Time (OT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OT} &= 900 - 90 - 40 \\
          &= 770 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\text{OT}}{\text{PPT}} = \frac{770}{900} = 0.8556 = 85.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Performance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{ICT} \times N}{\text{OT}} = \frac{0.1 \times 15000}{770} = \frac{1500}{770} = 1.9481
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Cap at 100% per OEE convention:**

$$
\therefore P = 1.00 = 100\%
$$

> **Diagnostic Insight (1 mark):** A performance ratio >1 indicates the *ideal cycle time assumption is wrong* — the machine's true design cycle is faster than 6 seconds, or the operating time data is incomplete. The plant must recalibrate its ideal cycle time.

**Step 5 — Quality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{G}{N} = \frac{14700}{15000} = 0.98 = 98.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — OEE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= A \times P \times Q \\
           &= 0.8556 \times 1.00 \times 0.98 \\
           &= 0.8385 \\
           &= 83.85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
> The plant's OEE of **83.85% is close to but below the world-class benchmark of 85%**. The plant is *near world-class* but held back primarily by the **Quality loss** (2% rejection) and **Availability loss** (14.44% of planned time lost to breakdowns and changeover). **Recommendations:** (i) Deploy SMED to reduce the 40-minute changeover toward single-digit minutes; (ii) implement Statistical Process Control to drive first-pass quality to 99%+; (iii) recalibrate the ideal cycle time to obtain a meaningful performance ratio.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q.B. (a)** Discuss the role of **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** and **Total Quality Management (TQM)** as twin pillars of WCM. Explain the **eight pillars of TPM** and the **PDCA cycle** in this context. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** A CNC turning cell has the following operating data for one week (5 days, 1 shift of 8 hours, with 45 minutes planned downtime per shift). Unplanned downtime: 5 hours 15 minutes. Setup/changeover time: 3 hours 30 minutes. Total pieces produced: 9,800. Ideal cycle time: 2 minutes. Defective pieces: 245. Calculate:
   (i) Availability, Performance, and Quality
   (ii) Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)
   (iii) State whether the cell qualifies as world-class and justify. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B.(a)

**TPM and TQM as Twin Pillars (2 marks):**
> TPM and TQM are the **operational heart of WCM**. TPM ensures that the *physical means of production* (machines) are reliable and available; TQM ensures that the *output* (processes and products) meets customer requirements. Neither alone is sufficient: a perfectly reliable machine producing defective parts, or a perfect process running on chronically failing equipment, both fail the WCM test. Together they form the **operational engine** that enables JIT (which has zero tolerance for downtime) and Continuous Improvement (which has zero tolerance for instability).

**The Eight Pillars of TPM (4 marks — ~0.5 mark each):**

1. **Autonomous Maintenance** — operators perform daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection of their own machines.
2. **Planned Maintenance** — scheduled, preventive, and predictive maintenance activities designed to eliminate breakdowns.
3. **Quality Maintenance** — using maintenance to *prevent* defects, not just failures; "zero defects through zero malfunctions."
4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)** — cross-functional teams tackling specific chronic losses using analysis tools.
5. **Early Equipment Management (EEM)** — designing new equipment with reliability and maintainability built-in from the start.
6. **Training & Education** — building a multi-skilled workforce capable of autonomous and planned maintenance.
7. **Office TPM** — extending TPM concepts (5S, visual management, process standardization) to administrative functions.
8. **Safety, Health, Environment (SHE)** — creating a zero-accident, zero-pollution workplace as a *prerequisite* for the other seven pillars.

**The PDCA Cycle in TQM (1 mark):**
> Deming's **PDCA** is the engine of continuous improvement:
> - **Plan** — identify the problem, analyze, propose a countermeasure.
> - **Do** — implement the countermeasure on a small scale.
> - **Check** — measure the results vs. expected outcomes.
> - **Act** — standardize if successful; iterate if not. The cycle restarts at a higher baseline.

**Synthesis (0.5 mark):**
> TPM's eight pillars and TQM's PDCA are **mutually reinforcing** — TPM provides the *stable base* (no breakdowns) on which PDCA improvements can be layered, while TQM provides the *analytical discipline* (data-driven problem solving) that TPM teams use to eliminate chronic losses.

**Keyword Mnemonic for Exam:** *"Au-Pl-Q-Fo-E-T-O-S"* — **Au**tonomous, **Pl**anned, **Q**uality, **Fo**cused improvement, **E**arly equipment, **T**raining, **O**ffice, **S**afety.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B.(b) — OEE Numerical

**Given Data:**
- 5 days × 1 shift × 8 hours = 2,400 minutes/week
- Planned downtime: 45 min/day × 5 = 225 minutes
- Unplanned downtime: 5 h 15 min = 315 minutes
- Setup/changeover: 3 h 30 min = 210 minutes
- N = 9,800 pieces
- ICT = 2 minutes
- D = 245 → G = 9,800 − 245 = 9,555

**Step 1 — PPT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PPT} &= 2400 - 225 = 2175 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — OT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OT} &= 2175 - 315 - 210 = 1650 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{1650}{2175} = 0.7586 = 75.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Performance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{2 \times 9800}{1650} = \frac{19600}{1650} = 11.88
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Cap at 100%:** $P = 1.00$ (clearly indicates the ideal cycle time is an *aggregate* figure, not per-piece; or that ICT is severely underestimated. The cell is producing well below its supposed design rate — recalibration needed.)

**Step 5 — Quality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{9555}{9800} = 0.9750 = 97.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — OEE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= 0.7586 \times 1.00 \times 0.9750 \\
           &= 0.7396 = 73.96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — World-Class Assessment:**
> The cell's OEE of **73.96% is significantly below the 85% world-class benchmark**. The cell is in the **"Average" zone (60–85%)** per the SEMATECH OEE classification. **Major loss category:** Availability (24.14% loss) — driven by 525 minutes of unplanned downtime and changeover combined. **Recommendations:** (i) aggressive TPM implementation — particularly *Planned Maintenance* to eliminate the 315-minute breakdown total; (ii) *SMED* program to compress the 210-minute changeover toward single-digit minutes; (iii) Quality Maintenance (Pillar 3) to lift first-pass quality from 97.5% to 99%+.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 (CSFs for WCM)**

- [ ] **Definition of WCM** — best-in-class, integrated, holistic; not a single tool.
- [ ] **Definition of CSFs** — Rockart (1979) — limited areas of strategic importance.
- [ ] **Seven CSFs (Mnemonic: "L-C-Q-J-T-H-T")** — Leadership, Customer Focus, Quality (TQM), JIT, TPM, HR, Technology.
- [ ] **OEE = A × P × Q** — world-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**.
- [ ] **Six Big Losses** — Downtime, Changeover, Reduced Speed, Idling, Defects, Startup.
- [ ] **Eight TPM Pillars** — Au, Pl, Q, Fo, E, T, O, S.
- [ ] **PDCA Cycle** — Plan, Do, Check, Act (Deming Wheel).
- [ ] **Takt Time** = Available Time ÷ Customer Demand (seconds/unit).
- [ ] **Inventory Turns** world-class ≥ 12/year.
- [ ] **DPMO** ≤ 3.4 for Six Sigma.
- [ ] **OTD** ≥ 98% for WCM.
- [ ] **JIT + TPM = inseparable** — JIT without TPM = catastrophic downtime.
- [ ] **Leadership is the gating CSF** — all others depend on it.
- [ ] **CSFs are interdependent, not sequential** — simultaneous deployment is mandatory.
- [ ] **Maturity Roadmap** — Chaos → Functional Stability → Integrated Systems → Lean+Six Sigma → World Class.
- [ ] **Always show the *why* and the *interlink* in 14-mark answers** — listing alone loses marks.

> **Last-mile advice:** When solving OEE problems, write down the *units* on every line. KTU 2024 valuators are instructed to deduct marks for ambiguous unit treatment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value Added Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT866 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Value Added Engineering # SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Value Engineering (VE)** is a *systematic, function-oriented, team-based* methodology that uses a disciplined, structured approach to analyze the **functions** of a product, process, or service with the objective of improving its **value** to the customer at the lowest **total life-cycle cost**, without sacrificing quality, reliability, performance, or safety. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):** > Value Engineering is a *value-added engineering strategy* that focuses on **maximising the value ratio (V = F/C)** by optimising the relationship between **Function (F)** and **Cost (C)** of a product/process, while maintaining required performance standards. ### The Fundamental Value Equation $$ V = \dfrac{F}{C} $$ Where: - **V** = Value (dimensionless index, dimensionless) - **F** = Function — the *worth* or *utility* delivered to the customer - **C** = Cost — the *total life-cycle cost* incurred to deliver that function > [!NOTE] > **Sibling Terms Used in KTU Papers:** > - **Value Analysis (VA)** — Same method applied to *existing* products/processes. > - **Value Engineering (VE)** — Method applied during *design/development* stage. > - **Value Management (VM)** — Broader strategic umbrella covering both VA and VE. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu" 🍽️ Imagine you walk into a restaurant. You are **hungry** (a *need*), and you order a *Veg Biryani* (a *product*). The biryani provides two key **functions**: - **Primary Function:** Satisfy your hunger (basic need). - **Secondary Function:** Provide aroma, taste, presentation. Now, suppose the restaurant reduces the quantity of rice but adds extra vegetables and spices. The biryani still *satisfies your hunger*, *tastes better*, but is **cheaper** to produce. $$\text{Your Value} = \frac{\text{Taste + Fullness}}{\text{Price Paid}} \uparrow \uparrow$$ | Parameter | Restaurant Analogy | Manufacturing Mapping | |---|---|---| | **Customer** | Hungry diner | End-user of the product | | **Need** | Hunger | Functional requirement | | **Product** | Biryani | Manufactured component | | **Function (F)** | Tastes good, fills stomach | What the product *does* | | **Cost (C)** | ₹ 250 | Manufacturing + life-cycle cost | | **Value (V)** | Satisfaction/₹ | Customer-perceived worth | **Intuition for a first-time reader:** Value Engineering is *not* about cutting cost alone, nor about adding fancy features. It is about making the product **do its job (function) better for every rupee spent**. Sometimes value increases by *raising F*, sometimes by *lowering C*, and ideally — both. --- ## 1.3 Historical Context (Frequently Asked in KTU) > [!NOTE] > **Origin of Value Engineering** > - Conceived by **Lawrence D. Miles** of *General Electric (GE)* during **World War II (1947)**. > - Trigger: Material shortages forced engineers to find cheaper substitutes that delivered **equal or better function**. > - First formal paper published in **1947** titled *"Value Analysis"*. > - **SAVE International** (Society of American Value Engineers, founded 1959) is the global certifying body. --- ## 1.4 Types of Value — The 8 Value Categories A KTU-favourite question lists the *types* of value. Memorise these: | # | Type of Value | Meaning | KTU Mnemonic | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Use Value (U)** | Worth derived from function/utility | **U**seful | | 2 | **Esteem Value (E)** | Worth derived from appearance/prestige | **E**xclusive look | | 3 | **Cost Value (C)** | Worth measured by what is paid | **C**urrency paid | | 4 | **Exchange Value (X)** | Resale/trade-in worth | e**X**change | | 5 | **Functional Value** | Ability to perform intended function | Default | | 6 | **Aesthetic Value** | Beauty, form, finish | Pretty | | 7 | **Social Value** | Social acceptability/status | Brand | | 8 | **Moral/Ethical Value** | Compliance with norms & ethics | Right thing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** When asked *"What value does VE primarily target?"* — answer **Use Value (Function per rupee)**, with **Esteem Value** as a secondary driver. --- ## 1.5 When to Apply VE? — The "VE Trigger Points" A KTU exam favourite. Apply VE when: - Cost is **rising** without function gain - Product design is **redundant** or **over-engineered** - Substitute materials are available - Multiple vendors offer the **same function** at different costs - Product is at **end-of-life** redesign stage - New product *concept* phase # SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Value Engineering Job Plan (The 6-Phase Standard) This is the **single most important framework** for any VE question. SAVE International mandates a structured, six-phase job plan: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Six-Phase VE Job Plan (Master Framework)** > 1. **Information Phase** — Gather facts, define problem. > 2. **Function Analysis Phase** — Identify *what* the product does (verb-noun). > 3. **Creative/Speculation Phase** — Brainstorm alternatives. > 4. **Judgment/Analysis Phase** — Evaluate alternatives, shortlist. > 5. **Development Phase** — Develop selected alternative into a recommendation. > 6. **Presentation/Implementation Phase** — Sell the idea to management, implement. ### Phase-Wise Detailed Breakdown | Phase | Question It Answers | Tools Used | Output | |---|---|---|---| | **1. Information** | *What is?* | Cost data, drawings, specs | Problem statement | | **2. Function Analysis** | *What does it do?* | FAST diagrams, verb-noun | Function model | | **3. Creative** | *What else could do it?* | Brainstorming, SCAMPER | Idea list | | **4. Judgment** | *Which is best?* | Cost-benefit, weighted matrix | Shortlist | | **5. Development** | *How to implement?* | Detailed cost study, prototype | VE Proposal | | **6. Presentation** | *Why should we adopt?* | Report, oral pitch | Approved VE change | --- ## 2.2 Function Analysis — The Heart of VE ### 2.2.1 The Two-Word Function Rule Every function must be expressed as **VERB + NOUN** — a discipline introduced by Lawrence Miles. | Product | ❌ Bad Function | ✅ Correct Function | |---|---|---| | Pencil | "Writes" | **Mark** + **Surface** | | LED Bulb | "Gives light" | **Emit** + **Light** | | Car brake | "Stops car" | **Reduce** + **Speed** | | Electric kettle | "Boils water" | **Heat** + **Water** | | Rivet | "Joins two plates" | **Hold** + **Sheets** | > [!NOTE] > **Rule:** Verb must be an *active, measurable* verb. Noun must be *quantifiable*. Avoid "provide", "offer", "do" — they are *passive* and hide real function. ### 2.2.2 Basic vs Supporting Functions - **Basic Function (B):** The reason the product exists. Cannot be removed. *(e.g., light bulb → emit light)* - **Supporting Function (S):** Makes the basic function work. *(e.g., light bulb → conduct electricity, dissipate heat)* --- ## 2.3 FAST Diagram (Function Analysis System Technique) > [!IMPORTANT] > **FAST Diagram — KTU Board Favourite (8–10 marks)** A **left-to-right logical model** built by answering *How?* moving right and *Why?* moving left. It contains: ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Scope Boundary | Critical Path | Why │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` - **Left side:** Higher-order *Why?* functions. - **Right side:** Lower-order *How?* functions. - **Top:** "When?" — scope/time limits. - **Bottom:** "How else could it be done?" — alternatives. ### FAST Diagram Component Symbols | Symbol | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | Box on **left wall** | Higher-order function (why) | "Provide illumination" | | Box on **critical path** | Basic function | "Emit light" | | Box on **right wall** | Lower-order function (how) | "Convert electricity" | | Box **below critical path** | Supporting function | "Conduct current" | | Vertical line `│` on right | *Why* direction | --- ## 2.4 Function Cost vs Function Worth — VE's Compass | Term | Definition | Symbol | |---|---|---| | **Function Cost (C)** | Current cost of delivering the function | $C_{function}$ | | **Function Worth (W)** | Lowest cost to deliver the function at *minimum spec* | $W$ | | **Value Index** | $V_i = W / C$ | If $V_i = 1$, perfect value; if $V_i < 1$, over-spending | The goal of VE: **Reduce C toward W without compromising F.** --- ## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are *Board-Exam Ready*. Memorise the table.** | # | Formula | Description | When Used | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = \dfrac{F}{C}$ | Basic value equation | Definition questions | | 2 | $V_i = \dfrac{W}{C}$ | Value Index (cost efficiency) | Function analysis | | 3 | $\text{Cost Savings} = C_{old} - C_{new}$ | Rupees saved | Problem-solving | | 4 | $\%\text{Savings} = \dfrac{C_{old} - C_{new}}{C_{old}} \times 100$ | Percentage savings | Numerical problems | | 5 | $\text{VE Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost of VE Study}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | When is VE worthwhile? | Economic feasibility | | 6 | $\text{Worth} = \text{Minimum acceptable spec cost}$ | Defining function worth | FAST analysis | | 7 | $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output Value}}{\text{Total Input Cost}}$ | VE-impacted productivity | Strategic questions | | 8 | $\text{LCC} = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{mnt} + C_{disposal}$ | Total Life-Cycle Cost | Strategic questions | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Pitfall:** Never confuse **Function Cost** (what you spend) with **Function Worth** (the *minimum* required spend). The gap is **VE's target reduction zone**. --- ## 2.6 Why VE Works — The Engineering Logic VE works because **traditional cost reduction is *attribute-based***, while VE is ***function-based***: | Traditional Cost Reduction | Value Engineering | |---|---| | Cuts cost *attribute-by-attribute* (material, labour) | Cuts cost *function-by-function* | | May reduce function → customer unhappy | Preserves function → customer happy | | Department-level (procurement, production) | Cross-functional team (design, QC, finance, marketing) | | Short-term focus | Life-cycle focus | | Single answer | Multiple alternatives evaluated | > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-line Board Answer:** *"VE improves the **value ratio** by maintaining or enhancing function (F) while reducing cost (C), through systematic function analysis and creative synthesis."* --- ## 2.7 The VE Team Composition (KTU Frequently Asked) A typical VE study team has **5–8 members** with: | Role | Responsibility | |---|---| | **VE Team Leader** | Facilitator, process owner (certified CVS — Certified Value Specialist) | | **Design Engineer** | Provides design constraints | | **Manufacturing Engineer** | Provides process know-how | | **Quality Engineer** | Maintains quality/standards | | **Cost Accountant** | Provides cost data | | **Purchasing/Marketing** | Vendor & customer view | | **Outside Specialist** | Domain expert when needed | # SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Basic Value Equation (Full Derivation) ### **Problem:** > A manufacturing component currently costs **₹ 500** to produce and delivers a customer-perceived function value of **₹ 750** (in customer utility units). After a VE study, the new design costs **₹ 450** while maintaining the same function value. Calculate: > (a) Old Value Index > (b) New Value Index > (c) Cost savings > (d) % improvement in value ### **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify variables.** We have: - Old cost: $C_{old} = 500$ ₹ - New cost: $C_{new} = 450$ ₹ - Function value (same before & after): $F = 750$ units > Note: The function value is preserved — that is the *defining principle* of VE. **Step 2 — Compute the old value index $V_{old}$.** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{old} &= \dfrac{F}{C_{old}} \\[4pt] V_{old} &= \dfrac{750}{500} \\[4pt] V_{old} &= 1.500 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the new value index $V_{new}$.** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{new} &= \dfrac{F}{C_{new}} \\[4pt] V_{new} &= \dfrac{750}{450} \\[4pt] V_{new} &= 1.667 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the absolute cost savings.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Savings} &= C_{old} - C_{new} \\[4pt] \text{Savings} &= 500 - 450 \\[4pt] \text{Savings} &= 50 \; \text{₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate percentage improvement in value.** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\Delta V &= \dfrac{V_{new} - V_{old}}{V_{old}} \times 100 \\[4pt] \%\Delta V &= \dfrac{1.667 - 1.500}{1.500} \times 100 \\[4pt] \%\Delta V &= \dfrac{0.167}{1.500} \times 100 \\[4pt] \%\Delta V &= 11.13\% \end{aligned} $$ ### **Result Summary:** | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Old Value | $V_{old}$ | **1.500** | | New Value | $V_{new}$ | **1.667** | | Cost Saving | $\Delta C$ | **₹ 50** | | % Value Improvement | $\%\Delta V$ | **11.13 %** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Function stayed at 750, but cost fell from 500 → 450. Value rose by 11.13% **without** any sacrifice in function. This is *classical Value Engineering*. --- ## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Function Worth & Value Index (Function-Level VE) ### **Problem:** > A *bracket* on a machine serves two functions: > - **F1 (Basic):** Hold shaft → cost **₹ 320**, function worth = **₹ 240** > - **F2 (Supporting):** Resist vibration → cost **₹ 180**, function worth = **₹ 100** > > After VE study, the *new bracket* uses a composite material. New costs: F1 = ₹ 270, F2 = ₹ 130. > Determine the **function-wise value index** before and after VE. ### **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the *old* value index for each function.** For F1 (Hold shaft): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{F1,old} &= \dfrac{W_{F1}}{C_{F1,old}} \\[4pt] V_{F1,old} &= \dfrac{240}{320} \\[4pt] V_{F1,old} &= 0.750 \end{aligned} $$ For F2 (Resist vibration): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{F2,old} &= \dfrac{W_{F2}}{C_{F2,old}} \\[4pt] V_{F2,old} &= \dfrac{100}{180} \\[4pt] V_{F2,old} &= 0.556 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the *new* value index for each function.** For F1 (Hold shaft) — new cost = ₹ 270: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{F1,new} &= \dfrac{W_{F1}}{C_{F1,new}} \\[4pt] V_{F1,new} &= \dfrac{240}{270} \\[4pt] V_{F1,new} &= 0.889 \end{aligned} $$ For F2 (Resist vibration) — new cost = ₹ 130: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{F2,new} &= \dfrac{W_{F2}}{C_{F2,new}} \\[4pt] V_{F2,new} &= \dfrac{100}{130} \\[4pt] V_{F2,new} &= 0.769 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute overall savings.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Cost Old} &= 320 + 180 = 500 \;\text{₹} \\[4pt] \text{Total Cost New} &= 270 + 130 = 400 \;\text{₹} \\[4pt] \text{Total Savings} &= 500 - 400 = 100 \;\text{₹} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \%\text{Savings} &= \dfrac{100}{500} \times 100 = 20\% \end{aligned} $$ ### **Result Matrix:** | Function | Worth | Old Cost | New Cost | Old $V_i$ | New $V_i$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | F1: Hold shaft | 240 | 320 | 270 | 0.750 | **0.889** | | F2: Resist vibration | 100 | 180 | 130 | 0.556 | **0.769** | | **Total** | 340 | 500 | 400 | 0.680 | **0.850** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Both $V_i$ moved *closer to 1* (the perfect-value benchmark), and total cost reduced 20% — confirming the VE study was successful. **F2 had the most "waste"** ($V_i = 0.556$) and therefore the greatest VE improvement opportunity. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison — VE vs Other Cost-Reduction Methods | Parameter | **Value Engineering** | **Cost Reduction** | **Kaizen** | **Six Sigma** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Focus | Function | Attribute/line item | Process | Variation/defects | | Time | Project-based (4–8 wks) | Continuous | Daily | Project | | Goal | Maximise V = F/C | Reduce spend | Eliminate waste (Muda) | Reduce variation | | Team | Cross-functional | Finance-led | All employees | Black/Green Belts | | Function preserved? | **Yes** | Not guaranteed | Sometimes | Not focus | | Trigger | New/redesign | Budget pressure | Always-on | Problem statement | | Tool | FAST, function analysis | Spend analysis | 5S, kanban | DMAIC, SPC | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When VE is *NOT* Suitable | # | Condition | Why VE Fails | |---|---|---| | 1 | Tight deadline (< 1 week) | VE needs deliberate function study | | 2 | Highly regulated product (medical implants, aerospace) | Safety specs cannot be relaxed | | 3 | Truly unique one-off product | ROI of VE study = 0 | | 4 | Customer explicitly specifies design | No function-freedom | | 5 | Product near end of life, no redesign | Cost of change > savings | --- ## 3.5 Decision Rule: Should You Run a VE Study? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Rule (Board-Favourite):** > Run a VE study if: > $$ > \text{Expected Annual Savings} \geq 10 \times \text{VE Study Cost} > $$ > If the ratio $< 5$, the VE study is not economically justified. Industry rule-of-thumb: **VE payback period should be ≤ 3 months.** # SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 The 6-Phase VE Job Plan — Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Phase 1: Information
Gather cost, design, customer data] --> B[Phase 2: Function Analysis
Define verb-noun functions] B --> C[Phase 3: Creative Phase
Brainstorm alternatives] C --> D[Phase 4: Judgment Phase
Evaluate and shortlist] D --> E[Phase 5: Development Phase
Build prototype, cost model] E --> F[Phase 6: Presentation
Submit VE Proposal to management] F --> G{Approved?} G -- Yes --> H[Implement VE Change] G -- No --> I[Iterate or Close] H --> J[Post-VE Audit
Verify Savings] ``` --- ## 4.2 FAST Diagram — Electric Kettle (Classic KTU Example) ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[Store Energy] --> P1[Hold Water] P1 --> P2[Heat Water] P2 --> P3[Convert Energy] P3 --> P4[Support Vessel] P4 --> P5[Provide Handle] S1[Conduct Electricity] -.-> P3 S2[Insulate Handle] -.-> P5 S3[Indicate Temperature] -.-> P2 WHY[Provide Hot Water] --- P0 HOW[Boil in 5 min] --- P4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this FAST diagram:** > - Moving **left-to-right** asks *"How?"* — *How do we store energy? → By holding water.* > - Moving **right-to-left** asks *"Why?"* — *Why convert energy? → To heat water.* > - **Solid boxes** = critical-path (basic functions). > - **Dashed boxes** = supporting functions (S1, S2, S3). --- ## 4.3 Function-Cost Matrix — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Product: Bracket] --> B[Function F1: Hold shaft] A --> C[Function F2: Resist vibration] A --> D[Function F3: Align components] B --> E[Cost C1 = 320 rupees] B --> F[Worth W1 = 240 rupees] B --> G[Value Index V1 = 0.75] C --> H[Cost C2 = 180 rupees] C --> I[Worth W2 = 100 rupees] C --> J[Value Index V2 = 0.56] D --> K[Cost C3 = 100 rupees] D --> L[Worth W3 = 90 rupees] D --> M[Value Index V3 = 0.90] G --> N[Target: Push Vi to 1.0] J --> N M --> N ``` --- ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Should We Run a VE Study? ```mermaid flowchart TD A[New Product or Redesign?] -->|No| B[Skip VE] A -->|Yes| C{Cost > Target?} C -->|No| B C -->|Yes| D{VE Study Cost < 10% of Savings?} D -->|No| B D -->|Yes| E[Form VE Team] E --> F[Run 6-Phase Job Plan] F --> G[Implement & Audit] ``` --- ## 4.5 VE vs Traditional Cost Reduction — Functional Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TRAD[Traditional Cost Reduction] T1[Material Cut] --> T2[Labour Cut] --> T3[Overhead Cut] end subgraph VE[Value Engineering] V1[Function Identified] --> V2[Function Costed] --> V3[Function Worth Found] --> V4[Alternative Designed] --> V5[Value Max V = F over C] end TRAD -.Risk: Function Loss.-> X[Customer Dissatisfied] VE -.Preserves Function.-> Y[Customer Satisfied + Lower Cost] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. Define Value Engineering. State the value equation.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Value Engineering is a systematic, function-oriented, team-based problem-solving methodology that seeks to improve the **value** of a product, process, or service by optimising the ratio of function to cost. $$ V = \dfrac{F}{C} $$ where **V** = Value, **F** = Function (customer-perceived utility), **C** = Cost (life-cycle). *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for clarity of terms.)* --- ### **Q2. List any six types of value with one-line meaning.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Use Value** — worth from utility/function. 2. **Esteem Value** — worth from appearance/status. 3. **Cost Value** — worth measured by what is paid. 4. **Exchange Value** — resale or trade-in worth. 5. **Aesthetic Value** — beauty, form, finish. 6. **Functional Value** — ability to do the intended job. *(½ mark each.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Full VE Study Model `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **six-phase Value Engineering Job Plan** with the tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The VE Job Plan is a *structured procedure* prescribed by SAVE International to conduct value studies systematically. **Phase 1 — Information Phase** *(1 mark)* - Collect all available data: design drawings, cost sheets, vendor data, customer feedback, quality specs. - Identify the *problem owner*, the *constraints*, and the *customer's true needs*. - Tool: cost analysis, market survey, Pareto chart on cost contributors. **Phase 2 — Function Analysis Phase** *(2 marks)* - Define every function using the **verb-noun rule**. - Separate into **basic** and **supporting** functions. - Build the **FAST diagram** showing function logic. - Tools: FAST, function analysis system technique, function-cost matrix. **Phase 3 — Creative (Speculation) Phase** *(1 mark)* - Generate as many *alternative solutions* as possible using brainstorming, SCAMPER, lateral thinking. - No criticism allowed; quantity over quality. **Phase 4 — Judgment (Evaluation) Phase** *(1 mark)* - Screen ideas by feasibility, cost, risk. - Use weighted scoring matrix and cost-benefit comparison. **Phase 5 — Development Phase** *(1 mark)* - Develop the *best* alternative into a fully-costed VE proposal. - Detail the materials, processes, risks, ROI. **Phase 6 — Presentation (Implementation) Phase** *(1 mark)* - Present proposal to decision-makers in a written report + oral pitch. - After approval, oversee implementation; conduct *post-VE audit*. > *(Marks allocated within totals of 7; examiner discretion on sub-division.)* --- **(b)** A manufacturer produces a *door hinge* at ₹ 80/unit delivering a function value of ₹ 120. After a VE study, the new cost is ₹ 64/unit while function value improves to ₹ 140 due to better material. Compute old and new value indices, savings, and % improvement in value. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Old Value Index.** $$ V_{old} = \dfrac{F_{old}}{C_{old}} = \dfrac{120}{80} = 1.500 $$ **Step 2 — New Value Index.** $$ V_{new} = \dfrac{F_{new}}{C_{new}} = \dfrac{140}{64} = 2.188 $$ **Step 3 — Cost savings per unit.** $$ \Delta C = 80 - 64 = 16 \; \text{₹/unit} $$ **Step 4 — % cost reduction.** $$ \%\Delta C = \dfrac{16}{80} \times 100 = 20\% $$ **Step 5 — % improvement in value.** $$ \%\Delta V = \dfrac{2.188 - 1.500}{1.500} \times 100 = 45.83\% $$ > **Conclusion:** Cost reduced 20%, function value improved 16.67%, and *overall value* rose by 45.83% — a textbook VE win where *both F↑ and C↓ simultaneously*. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]` **Q. (a)** Explain the **Function Analysis System Technique (FAST)**. Draw the FAST diagram for an **electric kettle**, clearly marking basic, supporting, and higher-order functions. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** FAST (Function Analysis System Technique) is a *graphical tool* used in the Function Analysis Phase of VE. It depicts the logical relationships between functions of a product/service using boxes and connecting lines, answering *"How?"* (left → right) and *"Why?"* (right → left). **FAST Diagram for an Electric Kettle (5 marks):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ "When?" ────────── Boil within 5 minutes ─────────────────────── │ │ │ │ │ "Why?" ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │ │ Provide │ Higher-Order│ → │ Basic │ → │ Lower-Order │ │ │ Hot Water │ Function │ │ Function │ │ Function │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ (Why?) │ Heat │ │ Heat │ │ Convert │ │ │ │ Water │ │ Water │ │ Electricity │ │ │ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ │ │ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ │ │ │ Store │ │ Contain │ │ Support │ │ │ │ Energy │ │ Water │ │ Vessel │ │ │ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ │ │ │ │ Supporting Functions (below critical path): │ │ • Conduct Electricity • Insulate Handle • Indicate Temperature │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Explanation (within 5 marks):** - **Critical Path (left to right):** Heat Water (basic) ← supported by → Store Energy, Contain Water. - **Supporting functions** (below critical path): Conduct Electricity, Insulate Handle, Indicate Temperature. - **Why?** — Why store energy? → To heat water. Why heat water? → To provide hot water. - **How?** — How do we heat water? → By converting electricity using a heating element. --- **(b)** Differentiate between **Value Analysis (VA)** and **Value Engineering (VE)**. State any **four situations** where VE is recommended. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Difference Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | Value Engineering (VE) | Value Analysis (VA) | |---|---|---| | Stage | Design / development | Existing product / process | | Goal | Pre-emptively improve value | Reactively reduce cost | | Team involvement | Design + cross-functional | Mostly production + cost | | Timing | *Before* product launch | *After* product is in market | | Result | New design | Re-design or re-spec | | Originator | Lawrence Miles, 1947 | Same; applied to existing lines | | Typical duration | 4–8 weeks | 2–4 weeks | **Four situations when VE is recommended (3 marks — ¾ mark each):** 1. **New product development** — VE at design stage ensures value built-in. 2. **Cost reduction targets unmet** by traditional methods. 3. **Product re-engineering / re-launch** for new markets. 4. **High-cost or high-volume components** where even 1% saving scales significantly. 5. *(Bonus)* Substitute materials available in market at lower cost. --- ## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING > [!WARNING] > **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in VE Questions** > 1. **Confusing VA and VE** — VA = existing product; VE = new design. Examiners deduct ½–1 mark each time. > 2. **Writing "V = F/C" without defining F and C** — Always *expand the symbols*; never assume the examiner knows. > 3. **Listing the 6 phases but missing the tools used in each** — Board examiners expect phase + tool + output triplet. > 4. **FAST diagram with no "Why/How" arrows** — Without the left-right logic, the FAST is just a flow chart and gets 1–2 marks out of 5. > 5. **In numerical problems, missing units (₹, %, units)** — Always show units in final answers. > 6. **Forgetting that VE preserves function** — saying VE *just reduces cost* loses a critical conceptual mark. > 7. **Naming only "cost reduction" instead of "value improvement"** as VE's goal — partial deduction. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Value Added Engineering** > - **Definition:** VE = systematic, function-oriented, team-based method to maximise V = F/C. > - **Originator:** Lawrence D. Miles, GE, 1947. > - **Body:** SAVE International (Society of American Value Engineers). > - **8 Types of Value:** Use, Esteem, Cost, Exchange, Functional, Aesthetic, Social, Moral. > - **6-Phase Job Plan:** Information → Function Analysis → Creative → Judgment → Development → Presentation. > - **Verb-Noun Rule:** Function must be expressed as **active verb + measurable noun**. > - **FAST Diagram:** Left-to-right = "How?", right-to-left = "Why?". Boxes on critical path = basic functions. > - **Value Equation:** $V = F / C$. > - **Value Index:** $V_i = W / C$ (perfect value when $V_i = 1$). > - **Cost Savings formula:** $C_{old} - C_{new}$; % Savings = $(C_{old} - C_{new})/C_{old} \times 100$. > - **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC):** $C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{mnt} + C_{disposal}$. > - **VE Decision Rule:** Run VE if *expected annual savings ≥ 10 × study cost*. > - **Team Size:** 5–8 cross-functional members; led by a *Certified Value Specialist (CVS)*. > - **VA vs VE:** VA is for *existing* products; VE is for *new/redesign* stage. > - **VE preserves function** — distinguishes it from crude cost-cutting. > - **Mnemonic for VE phases:** "**I**nformation **F**irst, **C**reative **J**udgment, **D**evelop **P**resent" → IFCJDP. > - **Key Tools:** FAST, brainstorming, SCAMPER, weighted scoring, cost-benefit analysis.

topics.title: Total Employee Involvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT866 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Total Employee Involvement # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Total Employee Involvement (TEI)** is a strategic management philosophy and operational practice in which **every individual in an organization — from the CEO to the shop-floor operator — is actively, continuously, and meaningfully engaged** in the pursuit of the firm's manufacturing and business objectives, particularly in the areas of quality, productivity, safety, cost reduction, and continuous improvement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Total Employee Involvement is the *third pillar* of the **House of Lean** (along with Just-in-Time and Jidoka) and a foundational pillar of Total Quality Management (TQM). It represents the human dimension of any world-class manufacturing strategy. Mathematically expressed as a strategic coverage index: $$ \text{TEI Index} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot E_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i} $$ where: - $N$ = total number of employees in the organization - $E_i$ = *engagement score* of the $i^{th}$ employee (on a normalized scale, typically 0 to 1) - $w_i$ = *strategic weight* assigned to employee $i$ based on role criticality ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Cricket Team** 🏏 > Imagine a cricket team. A *Total Employee Involvement* strategy is like a captain who: > 1. **Knows every player** (no one is invisible — even the 12th man has a role). > 2. **Empowers players** to take smart decisions on the field (not just follow robotic instructions). > 3. **Trains them continuously** in batting, bowling, and fielding. > 4. **Recognizes** a brilliant catch, a maiden over, or a gritty partnership. > 5. **Reviews matches** together to learn from wins *and* losses. > > A team with only the captain thinking and the rest just fielding will lose. A team where **everyone is mentally "in the game"** wins championships. TEI is the manufacturing equivalent of this — every worker is mentally "in the game" of quality and improvement. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in TEI > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Standard KTU-Highlight Metrics:** > - **Employee Engagement Score (EES):** typically **70%** is considered world-class (Gallup benchmark). > - **Suggestions per Employee per Year (SPEY):** world-class target is **≥ 30 suggestions/employee/year** with **≥ 60% implementation rate**. > - **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** improvement attributable to shop-floor kaizens. ## 1.4 Strategic Position in Manufacturing Strategy Total Employee Involvement sits at the intersection of three strategic frameworks: | Framework | Role of TEI | |-----------|-------------| | **Lean Manufacturing** | One of the 3 pillars of the House of Lean | | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | One of the 5 pillars (along with customer focus, process approach, fact-based decision making, and continuous improvement) | | **World-Class Manufacturing (WCM)** | The "People Pillar" — formally called the *Workplace Organization / Autonomous Maintenance* pillar in the WCM model | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Total Employee Involvement The implementation of TEI rests on **five interconnected pillars**. A failure in any one pillar collapses the system. ### Pillar 1: Empowerment (Decision Authority) - Frontline workers are given the **authority to stop production** (the *Andon* cord in Toyota Production System). - Empowerment flows top-down: management must formally **delegate** authority. - **Andon Cord Pull Rate** = number of times the cord is pulled per shift (a metric of psychological safety). ### Pillar 2: Teamwork (Small Group Activities) - Cross-functional and intra-functional teams tackle structured problems. - **Quality Circles (QCs)** — typically 6–10 members, meet weekly for 1 hour on company time. - **Kaizen Teams** — project-based, time-bound improvement teams. - **Autonomous Maintenance Groups** — operators maintain their own machines. ### Pillar 3: Training & Skill Development - Multi-skill matrix (*Ishikawa* or *Toyota Skill Matrix*). - Operators trained in **statistical process control, problem-solving (8D, A3), root cause analysis (5-Why, fishbone)**. - The **Training Effectiveness Ratio (TER)**: $$ \text{TER} = \frac{\text{Improvement in Performance after Training}}{\text{Investment in Training (cost \& hours)}} $$ ### Pillar 4: Recognition & Reward - Monetary: gain-sharing, profit-sharing, suggestion bonuses. - Non-monetary: "Employee of the Month", public recognition, career progression. - **Recognition Frequency Index (RFI)** — recognized events per employee per year. ### Pillar 5: Communication & Transparency - Daily huddles / morning meetings (5–10 minutes, standing). - Visual management boards (andon boards, KPI displays). - Two-way feedback channels: open-door policy, suggestion boxes, digital platforms. ## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of TEI > [!NOTE] > **KTU expects students to link TEI to classical management theories. Memorize the mapping below.** | Theory | Theorist | TEI Implication | |--------|----------|-----------------| | Hierarchy of Needs | **Maslow** | Workers need self-actualization, not just physiological paychecks | | Two-Factor Theory | **Herzberg** | Hygiene factors (pay, conditions) must be met; motivators (achievement, recognition) drive TEI | | Theory X vs Theory Y | **McGregor** | TEI requires a Theory Y manager — belief that workers *want* to contribute | | Expectancy Theory | **Vroom** | Motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence | | Job Characteristics Model | **Hackman & Oldham** | Skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, feedback drive internal motivation | ### 2.2.1 Vroom's Expectancy Equation (Critical for KTU) $$ M = E \times I \times V $$ where: - $M$ = *Motivation* to exert effort - $E$ = *Expectancy* — belief that effort will lead to performance (0 ≤ E ≤ 1) - $I$ = *Instrumentality* — belief that performance will lead to reward (0 ≤ I ≤ 1) - $V$ = *Valence* — value the employee places on the reward (typically −1 to +1) > **Implication for TEI:** A manager must ensure *all three* are non-zero. A high-involvement culture with poor rewards still fails (V = 0). A rich reward system with no skills fails (E = 0). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD** — The following table consolidates every formula KTU examiners may test for this topic. All numerical problems will reduce to one of these. | # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Typical World-Class Benchmark | Application | |---|------------------|----------|-------------------------------|-------------| | 1 | TEI Index | $\text{TEI} = \frac{\sum w_i E_i}{\sum w_i}$ | $\geq 0.80$ | Aggregate engagement measure | | 2 | Suggestions per Employee per Year | $\text{SPEY} = \frac{\text{Total Suggestions}}{\text{No. of Employees}}$ | $\geq 30$ | Idea-generation health | | 3 | Suggestion Implementation Rate | $\text{SIR} = \frac{\text{Implemented}}{\text{Submitted}} \times 100$ | $\geq 60\%$ | Trust in the system | | 4 | Vroom Motivation | $M = E \cdot I \cdot V$ | Maximize all 3 | Designing reward systems | | 5 | Training Effectiveness Ratio | $\text{TER} = \frac{\Delta \text{Performance}}{\text{Cost of Training}}$ | Maximize | ROI of learning | | 6 | Absenteeism Rate | $\text{AR} = \frac{\text{Absent Days}}{\text{Total Workdays}} \times 100$ | $\leq 2\%$ | Engagement proxy | | 7 | Employee Turnover Rate | $\text{ETR} = \frac{\text{Departures}}{\text{Avg. Headcount}} \times 100$ | $\leq 8\%$ | Retention indicator | | 8 | First-Pass Yield (TEI-attributable) | $\text{FPY} = \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}} \times 100$ | $\geq 99\%$ | Quality outcome of TEI | | 9 | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Minimize | Financial outcome | | 10 | Kaizen Savings | $S = \sum (\text{Cost}_\text{before} - \text{Cost}_\text{after})$ | Maximize | Monetary impact of TEI | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > **Industry Connection (write this in your KTU answer to score full marks):** > - **Toyota Production System (TPS):** TEI is the "Respect for People" pillar — every worker can pull the *Andon cord*. > - **Toyota reported > 1 million employee suggestions implemented per year** in some plants. > - **Wipro, Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki** have institutionalized TEI via TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) and TQM. > - In **defence manufacturing (HAL, BHEL)** and **PSUs**, TEI drives *suggestion schemes* mandated by the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) productivity councils. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Vroom's Expectancy Theory (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A shop-floor supervisor is designing a recognition scheme for operators. Each operator believes: > - With effort, the probability of meeting the production target is **0.8** (Expectancy). > - If the target is met, the probability of receiving a bonus is **0.7** (Instrumentality). > - The bonus of ₹5,000 is valued at **+0.6** by the operator (Valence). > > **Calculate (a) the motivation score, and (b) the percentage change in motivation if training raises Expectancy from 0.8 to 0.95.** ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Identify the variables from the question. $$ E = 0.8, \quad I = 0.7, \quad V = 0.6 $$ **Step 2:** Apply Vroom's Expectancy Equation. $$ M_1 = E \times I \times V $$ $$ M_1 = 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.6 $$ $$ M_1 = 0.336 $$ > **Interpretation:** A motivation score of **0.336** out of a maximum of 1.0 is *low* — the manager must intervene. **Step 3:** Compute the new motivation after training. $$ E_{\text{new}} = 0.95, \quad I = 0.7, \quad V = 0.6 $$ $$ M_2 = 0.95 \times 0.7 \times 0.6 $$ $$ M_2 = 0.399 $$ **Step 4:** Calculate the percentage change in motivation. $$ \%\Delta M = \frac{M_2 - M_1}{M_1} \times 100 $$ $$ \%\Delta M = \frac{0.399 - 0.336}{0.336} \times 100 $$ $$ \%\Delta M = \frac{0.063}{0.336} \times 100 $$ $$ \%\Delta M \approx 18.75\% $$ > **Answer:** The motivation increases by **18.75%** purely from skill training (raising E from 0.8 → 0.95), *with no change in pay*. This numerically proves the KTU point: **training is a high-leverage TEI intervention.** ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Suggestion Scheme Metrics > **Problem:** A KTU-affiliated engineering firm has **500 employees**. In a year, **8,750 suggestions** were submitted and **5,250** were implemented. Calculate (a) SPEY, (b) SIR, and (c) the total monetary savings if the average saving per implemented suggestion is **₹1,200**. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Compute Suggestions Per Employee per Year (SPEY). $$ \text{SPEY} = \frac{\text{Total Suggestions Submitted}}{\text{Number of Employees}} $$ $$ \text{SPEY} = \frac{8{,}750}{500} $$ $$ \text{SPEY} = 17.5 \text{ suggestions/employee/year} $$ > **Evaluation:** A SPEY of 17.5 is **below the world-class benchmark of 30** — TEI health is *moderate*. **Step 2:** Compute Suggestion Implementation Rate (SIR). $$ \text{SIR} = \frac{\text{Implemented}}{\text{Submitted}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{SIR} = \frac{5{,}250}{8{,}750} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{SIR} = 60.0\% $$ > **Evaluation:** SIR = 60% meets the world-class threshold. The system has *good follow-through*. **Step 3:** Compute total monetary savings. $$ S = (\text{Implemented Suggestions}) \times (\text{Avg. Saving per Suggestion}) $$ $$ S = 5{,}250 \times 1{,}200 $$ $$ S = \text{₹}63{,}00{,}000 $$ > **Equivalent:** ₹ **63 Lakhs per year** in savings — a powerful financial justification of the TEI programme. **Step 4:** Compute the per-employee productivity of ideas. $$ \text{Savings per Employee} = \frac{63{,}00{,}000}{500} = \text{₹}12{,}600/\text{employee} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Diagnosing TEI Failure Modes > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield KTU Table — memorize the mapping of *symptom → root cause → intervention*.** | Symptom (What you see) | Probable Root Cause (Why it happens) | Pillar Affected | TEI Intervention | |------------------------|--------------------------------------|-----------------|------------------| | Low SPEY (few suggestions) | Fear of criticism, Theory X manager | Communication & Empowerment | Idea campaigns, anonymous digital portals | | High SPEY, low SIR | "Suggestion graveyard" — no action | Recognition & Empowerment | Guaranteed 30-day response policy | | High absenteeism | Low valence, poor hygiene factors | Herzberg hygiene | Improve pay, working conditions | | Quality circles exist but inactive | No problem-solving training | Training | 8D / A3 / 5-Why workshops | | High turnover of engineers | No career growth, Theory Y violation | Recognition | Dual career ladders (technical + managerial) | | Andon cord never pulled | Blame culture, low psychological safety | Empowerment | "Mistake = learning" policy | | Suggestions accepted but not sustained | No follow-up kaizen, ownership gap | Teamwork | Convert suggestions to standard work | ## 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Cost-Benefit Justification of TEI > **Standard KTU 14-mark essay sub-part:** "Justify the cost of a Total Employee Involvement programme in a manufacturing firm." A rigorous TEI investment justification is built on the following identity: $$ \text{Net Benefit of TEI} = \text{Benefits from TEI} - \text{Cost of TEI Programme} $$ Expanding each term: $$ \text{Benefits} = S_{\text{kaizen}} + \Delta \text{COPQ}_{\text{reduction}} + \Delta \text{FPY}_{\text{revenue}} + \text{Retention Savings} $$ $$ \text{Cost} = C_{\text{training}} + C_{\text{coordination}} + C_{\text{recognition}} + C_{\text{consultants}} $$ Substituting: $$ NB = (S_{\text{kaizen}} + \Delta\text{COPQ} + \Delta\text{FPY} \cdot P \cdot Q) - (C_{\text{training}} + C_{\text{coord}} + C_{\text{rec}} + C_{\text{cons}}) $$ where $P$ = unit price and $Q$ = annual production volume. > **Practical insight:** Industry data shows that for every **₹1 invested in TEI, ₹3 to ₹5 is returned** within 18–24 months (BIS / Japan Productivity Council studies). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Five-Pillar Architecture of Total Employee Involvement ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["TEI STRATEGY
World-Class People Pillar"] nodeB["PILLAR 1
EMPOWERMENT
Authority to act"] nodeC["PILLAR 2

TRAINING
Multi-Skill Matrix

RECOGNITION

Non-Monetary"] nodeF["PILLAR 5
COMMUNICATION
Visual Boards
Daily Huddles"] nodeG["OUTCOMES
Quality, Cost, Delivery
Morale, Safety"] nodeH["FOUNDATION
Trust, Psychological
Safety, Theory Y"] nodeH --> nodeA nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeG ``` ## 4.2 The TEI Implementation Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR phase1["PHASE 1
SENIOR LEADERSHIP
COMMITMENT
Vision and Policy"] phase2["PHASE 2

MEASURE
AND REVIEW
SPEY SIR FPY"] phase1 --> phase2 --> phase3 --> phase4 --> phase5 --> phase6 --> phase7 --> phase8 phase8 -.Feedback Loop.-> phase1 ``` ## 4.3 The Vroom Expectancy Engine — Mapped to TEI Levers ```mermaid graph LR expectancy["E
EXPECTANCY
Effort to Performance"] instrumentality["I
INSTRUMENTALITY

VALENCE

MOTIVATION

FAIR REWARDS
and TRANSPARENCY"] lever3["TEI LEVER 3
