# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS). List any four components of FMS.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** is a highly automated, computer-controlled production system that can process a variety of part types in low-to-medium volumes with minimal manual intervention. It integrates CNC machine tools, automated material handling, and centralized computer control.

The four primary components are:
1. **Workstation Subsystem** (CNC machines, machining centers)
2. **Material Handling Subsystem** (AGVs, conveyors, robotic loaders)
3. **Tool Management Subsystem** (Central tool crib, ATC)
4. **Computer Control Subsystem** (DNC, cell controllers, MES integration)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC) and Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC) | Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale** | Small (2-3 machines) | Large (4+ machines) |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Material Handling** | Simple (robotic arm) | Complex (AGVs, conveyors) |
| **Control** | Single controller | Hierarchical DNC network |
| **Part Variety** | 5-10 part types | 10-30+ part types |
| **Investment** | Moderate | Very High |
| **Example** | A 2-machine cell with one robot | A 10-machine shop with AGV network |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] **ESE Module Choice Pattern:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions offer internal choice. Two alternatives (A and B) are provided for full coverage.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the various types of flexibility in manufacturing with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the benefits and limitations of implementing an FMS in a manufacturing organization. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution A(a): Types of Manufacturing Flexibility

Manufacturing flexibility is the ability of a system to adapt to changing conditions. The major types are:

1. **Machine Flexibility (7 Marks coverage begins):**
   - The capacity of a single CNC machine to produce a range of part types without extensive retooling.
   - *Example:* A CNC turning center that can produce shafts, pins, and bushings by simply changing the NC program and tooling.

2. **Process Flexibility:**
   - The ability to produce a specific part using multiple machines or multiple sequences of operations.
   - *Example:* A cubic part can be machined on a horizontal or vertical machining center.

3. **Product Flexibility:**
   - The ease with which new products can be introduced into the existing system.
   - *Example:* Adding a new bracket design to the FMS part family with minimal NC programming changes.

4. **Routing Flexibility:**
   - The ability to determine alternate paths for parts through the system to avoid bottlenecks.
   - *Example:* If Workstation 2 is down, the system can route the part through Workstations 3 and 4.

5. **Volume Flexibility:**
   - The ability to operate profitably at varying production volumes.
   - *Example:* Producing 50 units one month and 500 units the next month without reconfiguration.

6. **Expansion Flexibility:**
   - The modular design allowing additional machines or workstations to be added easily.
   - *Example:* Adding a fifth CNC machine to an existing 4-machine FMS.

### Model Solution A(b): Benefits and Limitations of FMS

**Benefits:**

1. **Reduced Lead Time:** Setup times drop from hours to minutes, cutting throughput time by 35-50%.
2. **Lower WIP Inventory:** Continuous flow reduces inventory holding costs by 60-80%.
3. **Higher Machine Utilization:** Achieves 75-85% utilization vs. 25-35% in job shops.
4. **Improved Quality:** Automated CMM inspection ensures Six Sigma-level quality.
5. **Increased Labor Productivity:** One operator can supervise multiple machines simultaneously.
6. **Enhanced Manufacturing Flexibility:** Quick changeover enables mass customization.
7. **Better Space Utilization:** Vertical integration and compact layouts reduce factory footprint.

**Limitations:**

1. **High Capital Investment:** Initial setup costs can range from ₹5 Crore to ₹50+ Crore.
2. **Complex Technology:** Requires highly skilled programmers and maintenance engineers.
3. **System Rigidity:** FMS is optimized for a specific part family; major product changes may require redesign.
4. **Vulnerability to Downtime:** A single failure in the central controller or AMHS can halt the entire system.
5. **High Planning Complexity:** Production scheduling, tool allocation, and AGV routing require sophisticated algorithms.
6. **Underutilization Risk:** If the part family volume is too low, the FMS may not achieve ROI targets.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a Flexible Manufacturing System. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two CNC machines in an FMS operate for 480 minutes per shift. Machine 1 spends 60 minutes on actual cutting, 20 minutes on tool change, 10 minutes on loading, and 15 minutes idle. Machine 2 spends 90 minutes on cutting, 25 minutes on tool change, 15 minutes on loading, and 20 minutes on setup verification. Calculate the machine utilization for each. Identify the bottleneck and suggest two ways to improve its utilization. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution B(a): Major Subsystems of FMS

The four major subsystems of an FMS are:

**1. Workstation Subsystem:**
- Contains CNC machining centers, turning centers, and special-purpose machines.
- Equipped with Automatic Tool Changers (ATC) and Automatic Pallet Changers (APC).
- Each workstation is controlled by a dedicated CNC controller.

**2. Material Handling & Storage Subsystem:**
- Uses Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs), Rail Guided Vehicles (RGVs), or conveyors.
- Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS) buffer parts between operations.
- Robotic loaders/unloaders interface the AMHS with machine tools.

**3. Tool Management Subsystem:**
- Centralized tool crib with RFID-based tracking.
- Tool pre-setters measure tool dimensions offline.
- Automated tool delivery ensures tools are at the right place at the right time.

**4. Computer Control Subsystem:**
- Hierarchical: Central Computer → Cell Controller → DNC → CNC Machine.
- Functions: NC program distribution, production scheduling, real-time monitoring, fault diagnosis.
- Interfaces with higher-level MES/ERP systems for production planning.

*(The block diagram was already covered in Section 4.1.)*

### Model Solution B(b): Machine Utilization Numerical Problem

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Machine 1 | Machine 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Total available time $T_a$ | 480 min | 480 min |
| Cutting time $T_{machining}$ | 60 min | 90 min |
| Tool change $T_{setup}$ | 20 min | 25 min |
| Loading time $T_{loading}$ | 10 min | 15 min |
| Idle time $T_{idle}$ | 15 min | — |
| Setup verification $T_{setup\_ver}$ | — | 20 min |

**Step 1: Calculate Utilized Time $T_u$ for Machine 1.**
$$T_{u,1} = T_{machining} + T_{setup} + T_{loading}$$
$$T_{u,1} = 60 + 20 + 10 = 90 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Machine Utilization $\eta_{m,1}$.**
$$\eta_{m,1} = \frac{T_{u,1}}{T_a} \times 100 = \frac{90}{480} \times 100$$
$$\eta_{m,1} = 18.75\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate Utilized Time $T_u$ for Machine 2.**
$$T_{u,2} = 90 + 25 + 15 + 20 = 150 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Machine Utilization $\eta_{m,2}$.**
$$\eta_{m,2} = \frac{150}{480} \times 100$$
$$\eta_{m,2} = 31.25\%$$

**Step 5: Identify the Bottleneck.**
The bottleneck is the machine with the **highest utilization** because it has the least spare capacity. Here, **Machine 2 is the bottleneck** with $\eta_{m,2} = 31.25\%$.

**Step 6: Suggestions to Improve Bottleneck Utilization.**
1. **Reduce Setup Verification Time:** Implement SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die) techniques to cut setup verification from 20 min to <5 min.
2. **Parallel Tool Pre-Setting:** Pre-set tools offline while the machine is cutting, eliminating the 25-min tool change delay.
3. **Add a Parallel Machine:** Install a second identical CNC machine to share the load.
4. **Optimize NC Programs:** Use high-speed machining strategies to reduce cutting time $T_{machining}$ from 90 min to ~70 min.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing FMS with FMC:** Students often interchange Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC) with FMS. Remember: FMC = 2-3 machines; FMS = 4+ machines with full AGV network.
> 2. **Forgetting Group Technology (GT):** FMS *requires* GT-based part families. Failing to mention GT in your answer is an automatic 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the Control Hierarchy:** The 5-level ISA-95 control hierarchy (from ERP down to sensors) is a frequent 3-mark sub-question. Memorize it.
> 4. **Missing Units in Numericals:** Always state the units (%, minutes, hours) in your final answer. Marks are deducted for unit-less answers.
> 5. **One-sided Analysis:** When asked about benefits/limitations, students often list only 3-4 points. Aim for 6-7 points each to secure full marks.
> 6. **No Diagram = 2 Marks Lost:** Every FMS question expects a labeled block diagram. Even a rough sketch earns partial credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist for Last-Minute Prep**

### 🔑 Core Concepts to Memorize:
- ✅ **FMS** = CNC Machines + Automated Material Handling + Computer Control.
- ✅ **Group Technology (GT)** is the *foundation* of FMS — parts must be classified into families.
- ✅ The **5-Level Control Hierarchy:** ERP → MES → Shop Floor → Cell/DNC → CNC/PLC → Sensors.
- ✅ **FMC** (2-3 machines) < **FMS** (4-10 machines) < **FML** (Flexible Manufacturing Line, >10 machines, dedicated).

### 📊 Key Performance Metrics:
- ✅ **Machine Utilization Target:** 75–85% (way above the 25–35% of conventional job shops).
- ✅ **WIP Reduction:** 60–80% compared to job shops.
- ✅ **Lead Time Reduction:** 35–50%.
- ✅ **Setup Time:** Cut from 45 min to <5 min using SMED.

### 🧠 The 8 Types of Flexibility (Mehrabi):
- ✅ Machine, Process, Product, Routing, Volume, Expansion, Operation, Production.

### ⚙️ Subsystems of FMS:
- ✅ Workstation | Material Handling | Tool Management | Computer Control.

### 🎯 KTU-Favorite Question Patterns:
- ✅ **"Define FMS and list components"** — 3 marks — Direct recall.
- ✅ **"Distinguish FMC vs FMS"** — 3-5 marks — Tabular comparison expected.
- ✅ **"Explain benefits/limitations"** — 7 marks — Use 6-7 bullet points each.
- ✅ **"Calculate machine utilization"** — 7 marks — Always show $T_u / T_a \times 100$ with units.
- ✅ **"Draw FMS block diagram"** — 7 marks — Show all 4 subsystems + data flow arrows.

### 🚫 Things to AVOID in Your Answer:
- ❌ Don't confuse FMS with a "CNC shop" — automation and integration are the differentiators.
- ❌ Don't ignore the **AMHS (Automated Material Handling System)** — it is the *circulatory system* of FMS.
- ❌ Don't forget to mention **Unmanned Operation / Lights-Out Manufacturing** capability.

### 📐 Formulas to Have on Your Fingertips:
- ✅ $\eta_m = \frac{T_u}{T_a} \times 100$
- ✅ $T_{th} = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{transport} + T_{wait}$
- ✅ Little's Law: $I = R \times T$
- ✅ $\eta_g = \left(1 - \frac{E}{Z}\right) \times 100$

> **🎓 Final KTU Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Detailed Explanation → Examples → Conclusion**. This mirrors the KTU marking scheme and maximizes score potential. Good luck!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toyota Production System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT866 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Toyota Production System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is an integrated socio-technical production system developed by **Taiichi Ohno**, **Eiji Toyoda**, and the Toyota Motor Corporation engineering team between **1948 and 1970**. It is formally defined as a comprehensive **Lean Manufacturing philosophy** that systematically eliminates waste (*Muda*), inconsistency (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*) to achieve the ultimate KTU-endorsed manufacturing objectives: **Highest Quality, Lowest Cost, and Shortest Lead Time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Expected):**
> *"The Toyota Production System is a holistic production framework that organises manufacturing and logistics for the automobile, including the interaction with suppliers and customers, to achieve minimum cost, maximum quality, and shortest lead time through the elimination of waste."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Flowing River

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The River of Production**
> Imagine your factory is a **shallow, fast-flowing river**. In a healthy river, water flows smoothly downstream; you can see the rocks (problems) at the bottom, and if a rock appears, everyone knows instantly. In an unhealthy river, deep pools of stagnant water (waste/inventory) hide problems. TPS says: **"Lower the water level."** When inventory (water) is reduced, problems (rocks) become visible, and the entire team can collectively remove them. *TPS is therefore a "Problem-Exposing and Problem-Solving System."*

## 1.3 The Three Evils of Production (Foundational Pillars)

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently award direct **3-mark questions** for precisely stating the "3M" framework below. Memorise verbatim.

| Term (Japanese) | English Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Muda** | **Waste** | Any activity that consumes resources without creating customer value (7 classical wastes) |
| **Mura** | **Unevenness** | Irregularity in production volume, flow, or workload that causes inefficiency |
| **Muri** | **Overburden** | Excessive strain placed on workers or equipment beyond natural limits |

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of TPS (House of Toyota Foundation)

1. **Just-in-Time (JIT)** — The right items, in the right quantity, at the right time.
2. **Jidoka (Autonomation)** — *Automation with a human touch* — Build in quality, stop and fix problems at the source.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** *"**J**idoka and **J**IT are the **JJ-Pillars**, and they rest on **Kaizen** (Continuous Improvement) and **Standardised Work**."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Architecture of TPS

TPS operates on **four concentric levels of strategic depth**:

### Level 1 — The Ultimate Goal
- Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time
- Safest, Highest Morale

### Level 2 — The Two Pillars
- **Just-in-Time (JIT)**
- **Jidoka (Autonomation)**

### Level 3 — The Mechanisms (How?)
- Continuous Flow
- Pull System (Kanban)
- Production Leveling (Heijunka)
- Built-in Quality (Poka-Yoke)
- Quick Changeover (SMED — Single Minute Exchange of Dies)
- Andon (Visual Signals)

### Level 4 — The Foundation (Cultural Bedrock)
- **Standardised Work**
- **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)**
- **5S Workplace Organisation**
- **Genchi Genbutsu (Go and See)**
- **Respect for People**

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Just-in-Time (JIT)
- Produces only **what is needed**, **when it is needed**, and **in the amount needed**.
- Creates a **Pull System**: downstream demand signals upstream production.
- Eliminates the classical **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD)**:
  - **T**ransportation
  - **I**nventory
  - **M**otion
  - **W**aiting
  - **O**verproduction
  - **O**ver-processing
  - **D**efects
  - (*Skills/Underutilised talent — added later as the 8th waste*)

### 2.2.2 Jidoka (Autonomation)
- Distinguished from simple automation by the inclusion of **human judgement**.
- Any abnormal condition (defect detected, machine overload) triggers an **automatic stop** and a **visual Andon signal**.
- Workers are empowered to **stop the line** (*Andon Cord*) to fix root causes.

### 2.2.3 Kanban — The Nervous System of JIT
- A **visual information system** using cards/electronic signals.
- Three Kanban types: **Withdrawal (Conveyance), Production (In-Process), and Vendor (Supplier) Kanban**.
- **Number of Kanban cards** governs Work-In-Progress (WIP) limit.

### 2.2.4 Heijunka (Production Leveling)
- **Levelling production by volume and mix** to prevent Mura.
- Aggregates demand over a time period (e.g., weekly) to smooth daily schedules.
- Even if a customer orders 1,000 units on Monday, the factory builds ~200/day.

### 2.2.5 Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)
- A culture of **small, incremental, daily improvements** by every employee.
- **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the formal engine.
- **5 Whys Analysis** is the standard root-cause tool.

### 2.2.6 Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing)
- Designing processes so that **errors are physically impossible** or instantly detected.
- *Example: USB connectors that only fit one way; car gear interlocks that prevent starting in gear.*

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA NOTATION RULE:** In all formulas below, the vertical bar denoting absolute value or "such that" is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to ensure clean LaTeX inside markdown tables.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $TT = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{customer}}}$ | The *pulse rate* of production. Sets the maximum allowable **cycle time per unit**. |
| 2 | **Number of Kanban Cards** | $N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1+S)}{C}$ | $D$ = demand rate, $L$ = lead time, $S$ = safety factor, $C$ = container size |
| 3 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality. World-class $\ge 85\%$ |
| 4 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Time}}$ | Measures **Muri** (breakdowns) |
| 5 | **Performance** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}}$ | Measures **Mura** (speed losses) |
| 6 | **Quality** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | Measures **Muda** (defect losses) |
| 7 | **Lead Time (LT)** | $LT = QT + WT + MT + ST + RT$ | $Q$ueue + $W$ait + $M$ove + $S$etup + $R$un time |
| 8 | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $VAR = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}}$ | Direct measure of waste. World-class VAR $\ge 25\%$ |
| 9 | **Inventory Turns (IT)** | $IT = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | JIT target: high turns (e.g., 30–100×) |
| 10 | **SMED Goal** | Internal $\rightarrow$ External | Convert all setup steps to external (machine running) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) often provides a numerical scenario and asks the student to compute **Takt Time** and the **Number of Kanban Cards**. Practise these two derivations exhaustively.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Toyota, Tesla, BMW, Ford — origin and benchmark.
- **Aerospace**: Boeing 787 production line uses TPS-derived Flow Lines.
- **Healthcare**: Virginia Mason Medical Center ("Toyota of Healthcare") — patient flow.
- **Software**: Lean-Agile (SAFe framework) is a direct descendant of TPS pull systems.
- **Construction**: Last Planner System pulls from TPS Heijunka principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Takt Time

> [!NOTE]
> Takt is a German word meaning "rhythm" or "musical beat." It is the **metronome** of an entire production line.

### Step 1 — Identify Available Production Time
For a single 8-hour shift (480 minutes), we **subtract mandatory non-productive intervals** (breaks, meetings, changeovers, and start-up). The remaining time is the *Net Available Time*.

$$T_{\text{available}} = T_{\text{shift}} - T_{\text{breaks}} - T_{\text{meetings}} - T_{\text{changeover}}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Customer Demand
The total number of units the customer has ordered (or the production plan commits to) over the **same time horizon**.

$$D_{\text{customer}} = \text{Units required per planning period}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Takt Time Formula

$$TT = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{customer}}}$$

**Units check:** $\dfrac{\text{seconds}}{\text{unit}}$ (or minutes/unit).

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Module 4, Internal Exam Pattern):**
> A Toyota assembly cell operates for **one shift of 8 hours**. The shift includes a **30-minute lunch break**, a **10-minute team huddle**, and a planned **20-minute SMED changeover**. The daily customer demand from the dealer network is **240 vehicles**.
> **(a)** Compute the **Takt Time** in seconds.
> **(b)** If the workstation cycle time is currently **85 seconds**, comment on **line balance**.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Convert shift to seconds:**

$$T_{\text{shift}} = 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \times 60 \text{ sec/min} = 28{,}800 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step B — Convert break deductions to seconds:**

$$T_{\text{breaks}} = 30 \times 60 = 1{,}800 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{\text{meetings}} = 10 \times 60 = 600 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{\text{changeover}} = 20 \times 60 = 1{,}200 \text{ s}$$

**Step C — Sum the deductions and compute Available Time:**

$$T_{\text{deductions}} = 1{,}800 + 600 + 1{,}200 = 3{,}600 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{\text{available}} = 28{,}800 - 3{,}600 = 25{,}200 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step D — Apply the Takt Time formula:**

$$TT = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{customer}}} = \dfrac{25{,}200}{240} = 105 \text{ seconds/unit}$$

**Step E — Line Balance Interpretation:**

$$\text{Cycle Time} = 85 \text{ s} \quad ; \quad \text{Takt Time} = 105 \text{ s}$$

Since **Cycle Time (85 s) $<$ Takt Time (105 s)**, the workstation has **20 seconds of slack** per vehicle. This is a **healthy line** with spare capacity to absorb variability. The cell can be loaded with additional tasks (Heijunka) or, conversely, demand can grow before bottleneck occurs.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's rule of thumb:** If the question says "the cell *must* meet demand," Takt is the **upper limit**; Cycle time must be **strictly less than or equal** to Takt time for sustainable flow.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Number of Kanban Cards

### Step 1 — Understand the Variables

- $D$ = **Demand rate** (units per unit time, e.g., units/hour)
- $L$ = **Lead time** to replenish one container (in same time units)
- $C$ = **Container capacity** (units per container)
- $S$ = **Safety factor** (decimal, e.g., 0.10 for 10% safety stock)

### Step 2 — The Formula

$$N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$$

**Logical origin:** $D \cdot L$ gives the demand during lead time. Multiplying by $(1+S)$ adds safety buffer. Dividing by $C$ converts units to number of containers (cards).

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A supplier delivers components to the assembly line. The line consumes **50 units per hour**. Lead time from the supplier is **4 hours**. Each container holds **25 units**. Management has set a **10% safety factor**.
> Compute the number of Kanban cards required.

**Solution:**

**Step A — List the parameters:**

$$D = 50 \text{ units/hr} \quad ; \quad L = 4 \text{ hr} \quad ; \quad C = 25 \text{ units} \quad ; \quad S = 0.10$$

**Step B — Compute demand during lead time:**

$$D \cdot L = 50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ units}$$

**Step C — Apply safety factor:**

$$D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S) = 200 \times 1.10 = 220 \text{ units}$$

**Step D — Convert to number of containers:**

$$N = \dfrac{220}{25} = 8.8 \approx 9 \text{ cards}$$

**Step E — Practical interpretation:** Since a **partial card is impossible**, the system requires **9 Kanban cards** to circulate. The 9th card represents the **safety buffer** of 5 units (since 8 cards would cover only 200 units).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Writing $N = 8.8$ as a final answer. **Always round UP** to the next integer — you cannot have 0.8 of a card in a physical system.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

### Step 1 — The Master Formula

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

### Step 2 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A stamping press has the following data for one shift:
> - **Planned Production Time:** 480 minutes
> - **Downtime (breakdowns + setup):** 60 minutes
> - **Ideal Cycle Time:** 0.5 minutes/piece
> - **Total Pieces Produced:** 720 pieces
> - **Defective Pieces:** 12 pieces
>
> **Compute OEE.**

**Solution:**

**Step A — Availability:**

$$A = \dfrac{480 - 60}{480} = \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875 \quad (87.5\%)$$

**Step B — Performance:**

$$\text{Run Time} = 420 \text{ min}; \quad \text{Ideal Pieces} = 0.5 \times 720 = 360 \text{ min wait — recompute carefully.}$$

Correct Performance calculation uses actual pieces and run time:

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 720}{420} = \dfrac{360}{420} = 0.857 \quad (85.7\%)$$

**Step C — Quality:**

$$Q = \dfrac{720 - 12}{720} = \dfrac{708}{720} = 0.9833 \quad (98.33\%)$$

**Step D — Master OEE Calculation:**

$$OEE = 0.875 \times 0.857 \times 0.9833 = 0.7372 \approx 73.7\%$$

**Step E — KTU Interpretation:**

> A world-class OEE is **$\geq 85\%$** (industry standard by SEMI/TPM). The press is operating **below world-class** but within typical Indian automotive Tier-1 supplier range. The **Performance loss (14.3%)** is the largest contributor and is best addressed by eliminating **Minor Stoppages** and **Reduced Speed Losses** (both classic Muda in OEE taxonomy).

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-yield board table. Memorise the *mapping* of problems to TPS tools.

| Problem Observed | Lean Diagnosis | TPS Tool to Deploy | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Excess WIP piled between machines | **Muda (Inventory)** + Mura | **Kanban / Continuous Flow** | Reduced WIP, visible constraints |
| Frequent machine breakdowns | **Muri (Overburden)** | **Jidoka + TPM + Poka-Yoke** | Equipment restored, autonomous stops |
| Unstable daily production schedule | **Mura (Unevenness)** | **Heijunka + Level Scheduling** | Smoothed takt-based output |
| High defect rate at end of line | **Muda (Defects)** | **Jidoka + Andon + Poka-Yoke** | Defects caught at source, zero pass-through |
| Workers afraid to stop the line | **Cultural barrier** | **Respect for People + 5 Whys** | Psychological safety, root cause learning |
| Setup times exceeding 30 minutes | **Muda (Waiting)** | **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies)** | Setup under 10 minutes, smaller batches |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The House of Toyota — Conceptual Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the canonical **House of Toyota** framework. This is a guaranteed **7-mark diagram** if asked in KTU 2024 ESE.

```mermaid
graph TB
    TPS_TOP[TOYOTA PRODUCTION SYSTEM]
    GOAL_NODE[Ultimate Goal: Best Quality Lowest Cost Shortest Lead Time]
    
    PILLAR_JIT[PILLAR 1 JUST IN TIME]
    PILLAR_JID[PILLAR 2 JIDOKA Automation with Human Touch]
    
    MECH_KAN[Kanban Pull System]
    MECH_HEI[Heijunka Production Leveling]
    MECH_FLO[Continuous Flow]
    MECH_SMD[SMED Quick Changeover]
    MECH_AND[Andon Visual Signals]
    MECH_POK[Poka Yoke Mistake Proofing]
    MECH_STP[Stop at Defect Authority]
    MECH_QAS[Quality at Source]
    
    FOUND_STW[Standardised Work]
    FOUND_KAI[Kaizen Continuous Improvement]
    FOUND_5SS[5S Workplace Organisation]
    FOUND_GGB[Genchi Genbutsu Go and See]
    FOUND_RFP[Respect for People]
    
    TPS_TOP --> GOAL_NODE
    TPS_TOP --> PILLAR_JIT
    TPS_TOP --> PILLAR_JID
    TPS_TOP --> FOUND_STW
    TPS_TOP --> FOUND_KAI
    TPS_TOP --> FOUND_5SS
    TPS_TOP --> FOUND_GGB
    TPS_TOP --> FOUND_RFP
    
    PILLAR_JIT --> MECH_KAN
    PILLAR_JIT --> MECH_HEI
    PILLAR_JIT --> MECH_FLO
    PILLAR_JIT --> MECH_SMD
    
    PILLAR_JID --> MECH_AND
    PILLAR_JID --> MECH_POK
    PILLAR_JID --> MECH_STP
    PILLAR_JID --> MECH_QAS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two pillars (*JIT* and *Jidoka*) **support** the roof (the *Ultimate Goal*). The foundation blocks (*Standardised Work, Kaizen, 5S, Genchi Genbutsu, Respect for People*) **hold up** the pillars. If the foundation is weak, the pillars collapse, and the goal is unattainable.

## 4.2 The TPS Pull-Flow Operational Map

This block-level flow shows how a customer order ripples through the entire TPS value stream.

```mermaid
graph LR
    CUST_NODE[CUSTOMER ORDER]
    ASSY_NODE[Final Assembly Stage]
    SUB_ASY[Sub Assembly Stage]
    FAB_NODE[Fabrication Stage]
    SUP_NODE[Supplier]
    
    K1[Kanban Signal Withdrawal]
    K2[Kanban Signal Production]
    K3[Kanban Signal Vendor]
    
    ANDON_NODE[Andon Alert Defect Detected]
    FIX_NODE[5 Whys Root Cause]
    KAIZEN_NODE[Kaizen Improvement]
    
    CUST_NODE -->|Demand Pull| ASSY_NODE
    ASSY_NODE -->|K1| SUB_ASY
    SUB_ASY -->|K2| FAB_NODE
    FAB_NODE -->|K3| SUP_NODE
    
    ASSY_NODE -.->|Defect Found| ANDON_NODE
    ANDON_NODE --> FIX_NODE
    FIX_NODE --> KAIZEN_NODE
    KAIZEN_NODE -.->|Improved Process| ASSY_NODE
```

## 4.3 The Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD) Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TB
    WASTE_ROOT[THE 7 WASTES TIMWOOD]
    W1[Transportation Unnecessary movement of materials]
    W2[Inventory Excess raw WIP and finished goods]
    W3[Motion Unnecessary movement of people]
    W4[Waiting Idle time of people or machines]
    W5[Overproduction Producing more than needed]
    W6[Over Processing Doing more work than customer values]
    W7[Defects Rework and scrap]
    
    WASTE_ROOT --> W1
    WASTE_ROOT --> W2
    WASTE_ROOT --> W3
    WASTE_ROOT --> W4
    WASTE_ROOT --> W5
    WASTE_ROOT --> W6
    WASTE_ROOT --> W7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the Toyota Production System (TPS). Explain the concept of "Jidoka."**

**Model Answer:**

The Toyota Production System (TPS) is a comprehensive **lean manufacturing philosophy** developed by Toyota Motor Corporation, designed to achieve the highest quality, lowest cost, and shortest lead time by systematically eliminating the three evils of production: **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), and **Muri** (overburden).

**Jidoka** (often translated as "autonomation") is the second pillar of TPS. It means **automation with a human touch** — the system is designed so that any abnormal condition (defect detection, tool wear, machine overload) automatically **stops the line** and triggers a visual **Andon signal**. This empowers workers to fix problems at the source, **build in quality**, and prevent defective items from being passed to the next process. *Jidoka* separates Toyota's automation from Western automation, which only detects *output* but not *process abnormality*.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for TPS definition + 2 marks for Jidoka with Andon linkage)*

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between JIT and traditional mass production. State any two advantages of JIT.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Traditional Mass Production | Just-in-Time (JIT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Inventory Philosophy** | Large buffer stocks to absorb variability | Minimum inventory, expose variability |
| **Flow Direction** | Push system (forecast-driven) | Pull system (demand-driven) |
| **Lot Size** | Large economic batches | Small, frequent deliveries |
| **Lead Time** | Long (often weeks/months) | Short (hours/days) |
| **Quality Control** | Inspection at end (rework) | Built-in quality at every station |
| **Supplier Relations** | Adversarial, many suppliers | Long-term partnership, few suppliers |

**Two Advantages of JIT:**
1. **Reduced inventory carrying costs** — less capital tied up in WIP, lower warehousing expenses, and reduced obsolescence risk.
2. **Improved quality and faster feedback** — small lot sizes surface defects immediately, enabling rapid root-cause analysis and continuous improvement.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for clear differentiation table + 1 mark per advantage)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE 2024 Part B questions on Module 4 follow a strict pattern: **either** descriptive theory **or** numerical/analytical. Below are **two alternative full-14-mark questions** providing the KTU internal-choice flexibility.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a) Explain in detail the two pillars of the Toyota Production System. Describe the House of Toyota framework with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The Toyota plant in Bidadi operates for 2 shifts of 8 hours each. Each shift includes a 45-minute lunch break, a 10-minute morning huddle, and 15 minutes of SMED changeover. The daily customer demand is 540 cars. Compute the Takt Time. If the workstation cycle time is 100 seconds, comment on the line balance. Also calculate the number of Kanban cards required for a sub-assembly line that consumes 80 units/hour, has a supplier lead time of 6 hours, a container size of 40 units, and a safety factor of 12%. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The Toyota Production System rests on **two pillars** that support the ultimate goal of *Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time*:

**Pillar 1 — Just-in-Time (JIT):**
JIT is a pull-based production philosophy that produces **only what is needed, when it is needed, and in the amount needed**. Its operational mechanisms include:
- **Continuous flow** to link processes seamlessly.
- **Kanban** (visual cards) to signal withdrawal and production.
- **Heijunka** (production leveling) to prevent Mura.
- **SMED** (Single Minute Exchange of Dies) to enable small batches.

**Pillar 2 — Jidoka (Autonomation):**
Jidoka is "automation with a human touch." It empowers machines and workers to **detect abnormalities and stop production** autonomously. Key elements include:
- **Andon** (visual signal board).
- **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing devices).
- **Authority to stop the line** (*Andon Cord*).

**House of Toyota Framework:**
The roof represents the *Ultimate Goal*. The two pillars (*JIT* and *Jidoka*) support it. The foundation consists of *Standardised Work, Kaizen, 5S, Genchi Genbutsu,* and *Respect for People*. *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual block representation.)*

*(Distribution: 2 marks each pillar, 1 mark for House of Toyota description, 2 marks for diagram)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Convert total time to seconds:**

$$T_{\text{shift,total}} = 2 \text{ shifts} \times 8 \text{ hr/shift} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 57{,}600 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute deductions per shift (×2 shifts):**

$$T_{\text{lunch}} = 45 \times 60 \times 2 = 5{,}400 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{\text{huddle}} = 10 \times 60 \times 2 = 1{,}200 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{\text{SMED}} = 15 \times 60 \times 2 = 1{,}800 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Net Available Time:**

$$T_{\text{available}} = 57{,}600 - 5{,}400 - 1{,}200 - 1{,}800 = 49{,}200 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Takt Time:**

$$TT = \dfrac{49{,}200}{540} = 91.11 \text{ seconds/car}$$

**Step 5 — Line Balance:**

Cycle Time (100 s) $>$ Takt Time (91.11 s). This indicates a **bottleneck** — the workstation **cannot keep pace** with customer demand. **Recommendations:** reduce cycle time through work-balancing, motion study, or adding parallel resources.

**Step 6 — Number of Kanban Cards:**

$$N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} = \dfrac{80 \times 6 \times 1.12}{40} = \dfrac{537.6}{40} = 13.44$$

$$\text{Rounded up: } N = 14 \text{ cards}$$

*(Distribution: 2 marks Takt calculation, 1 mark line balance, 1 mark Kanban formula application, 1 mark rounding, 2 marks for explanation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **multiply deductions by 2 shifts** in part (b). This single error propagates to wrong Takt time, wrong line balance, and lost marks downstream. **Always re-read** the problem statement for the *number of shifts*. One mark is reserved for this precision.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) Define Muda, Mura, and Muri. Explain the 7 classical wastes (TIMWOOD) of TPS with one engineering example for each. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A CNC machining centre has the following daily data: Planned Production Time = 600 minutes, Downtime = 90 minutes, Ideal Cycle Time = 1.2 minutes/piece, Total Pieces Produced = 380, Defective Pieces = 19. Calculate OEE and identify which of the three OEE components is the weakest link. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

- **Muda:** Any activity that consumes resources without creating customer value. (1 mark)
- **Mura:** Unevenness in production volume, mix, or workload. (1 mark)
- **Muri:** Overburdening of people or equipment beyond natural limits. (1 mark)

**The 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD):**

| # | Waste | Definition | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **T**ransportation | Unnecessary movement of materials | Moving parts 500 m between cells when 50 m layout is possible |
| 2 | **I**nventory | Excess raw/WIP/finished goods | 30 days of WIP stored between stamping and welding |
| 3 | **M**otion | Unnecessary movement of people | Worker reaching 2 m to grab a tool instead of 0.5 m |
| 4 | **W**aiting | Idle time of people/machines | CNC machine idle for 40 min waiting for operator |
| 5 | **O**verproduction | Producing more than customer needs | Making 1,000 units when order is 600 — leads to inventory waste |
| 6 | **O**ver-processing | Doing more work than the customer values | Machining a surface to 0.1 µm Ra when drawing specifies 1.6 µm |
| 7 | **D**efects | Rework and scrap | 5% defect rate at end of line requiring 100% inspection |

*(Distribution: 0.5 mark per waste definition + 0.5 mark per engineering example = 7 marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Availability (A):**

$$A = \dfrac{600 - 90}{600} = \dfrac{510}{600} = 0.85 \quad (85.0\%)$$

**Step 2 — Performance (P):**

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Run Time}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 380}{510} = \dfrac{456}{510} = 0.8941 \quad (89.4\%)$$

**Step 3 — Quality (Q):**

$$Q = \dfrac{380 - 19}{380} = \dfrac{361}{380} = 0.95 \quad (95.0\%)$$

**Step 4 — OEE:**

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.85 \times 0.8941 \times 0.95 = 0.7219 \approx 72.2\%$$

**Step 5 — Weakest Link Identification:**

| Component | Value | Loss |
|---|---|---|
| Availability | 85.0% | 15.0% loss |
| Performance | 89.4% | 10.6% loss |
| Quality | 95.0% | **5.0% loss** |

**Quality is the strongest, but Availability has the largest loss (15%)** when measured in absolute percentage points. The **weakest link is Availability (A)**, indicating the **biggest opportunity for improvement** lies in reducing unplanned downtime (breakdowns, setup) — typically addressed by **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** and **Jidoka** mechanisms.

*(Distribution: 1.5 marks each for A, P, Q; 1 mark for OEE multiplication; 1.5 marks for weakest link analysis with reasoning)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Errors:**
> 1. **Performance Formula Confusion:** Students frequently write $P = \dfrac{\text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Ideal Pieces}}$ which is dimensionally wrong. The correct formula uses **Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces in numerator** and **Run Time in denominator**.
> 2. **Weakest Link Misidentification:** Don't just say "Quality is low." Always provide the **percentage loss** to justify your ranking. State *"Availability has the largest absolute loss of 15%."*
> 3. **Forgetting the OEE Master Formula:** $A \times P \times Q$ is **not additive** — never add the components.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Memory Recall Sheet for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ✅ **TPS Originators:** Taiichi Ohno + Eiji Toyoda (post-WWII, 1948–1970).
- ✅ **Ultimate Goal:** Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time, Safest, Highest Morale.
- ✅ **Two Pillars:** JIT (pull, flow, Kanban) + Jidoka (stop-on-defect, Andon).
- ✅ **Three Evils (3M):** Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden).
- ✅ **7 Wastes Mnemonic:** **TIMWOOD** — Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- ✅ **Takt Time Formula:** $TT = \dfrac{T_{\text{available}}}{D_{\text{customer}}}$ (always in time-per-unit).
- ✅ **Kanban Formula:** $N = \dfrac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}$ — **always round UP** to the next integer.
- ✅ **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ — world-class $\geq 85\%$.
- ✅ **Kanban Types:** Withdrawal, Production, Vendor (Supplier) Kanban.
- ✅ **5S:** Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardise, Sustain.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing — make errors physically impossible.
- ✅ **SMED:** Single Minute Exchange of Dies — convert internal setup to external.
- ✅ **Heijunka:** Level production by volume and mix to prevent Mura.
- ✅ **Kaizen:** Continuous incremental improvement (small, daily, by everyone).
- ✅ **Genchi Genbutsu:** "Go and See" — direct observation at the *gemba* (actual place).
- ✅ **Andon:** Visual signal board indicating line status (green/yellow/red).
- ✅ **Value-Added Ratio:** $VAR = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}}$ — World-class $\geq 25\%$.
- ✅ **Lead Time:** $LT = QT + WT + MT + ST + RT$.
- ✅ **Line Balance Rule:** Cycle Time $\leq$ Takt Time for sustainable flow.
- ✅ **House of Toyota:** Roof = Goal; Pillars = JIT + Jidoka; Foundation = Standardised Work, Kaizen, 5S, Genchi Genbutsu, Respect for People.
- ✅ **14 Principles of Toyota Way:** Divided into 4P model — Philosophy, Process, People/Partners, Problem Solving.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Toyota Production System**
> *You are now equipped with the technical depth, formula fluency, and KTU-aligned question-pattern readiness to score full marks in any TPS-related 3-mark or 14-mark question on the 2024 ESE paper. Practise the three numerical derivations (Takt, Kanban, OEE) at least twice before the exam.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Class Manufacturing and India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT866 - MANUFACTURING STRATEGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** World Class Manufacturing and India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World Class Manufacturing (WCM)** is defined as the state of excellence in manufacturing operations wherein an enterprise achieves **best-in-class performance** across all critical dimensions — quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and innovation — by deploying an integrated system of modern management philosophies, advanced technologies, lean production principles, total quality management, and continuous improvement practices.

According to **Schonberger (1986)**, WCM refers to the combination of Japanese manufacturing techniques (such as **Just-in-Time, Total Quality Control, Total Productive Maintenance, and Kaizen**) with the strategic intent of achieving world-class standards in productivity, quality, and customer responsiveness.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: WCM is NOT a single technique — it is an **integrative paradigm** that absorbs and harmonises multiple world-class frameworks (TQM, Lean, Six Sigma, TPM, BPR, Agile Manufacturing, Industry 4.0).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Olympic Athlete of Manufacturing**
>
> Imagine a factory is like an **Olympic sprinter**. To win gold (become "World Class"), the athlete must excel simultaneously in:
> - **Speed** (Cycle Time / Lead Time reduction)
> - **Stamina** (Equipment uptime & reliability — **OEE > 85%**)
> - **Form** (Defect-free output — **Six Sigma Quality, < 3.4 DPMO**)
> - **Coachability** (Employee empowerment, Kaizen culture)
> - **Adaptability** (Quick changeover — **SMED < 10 minutes**)
>
> A world-class factory, like an Olympic athlete, does not merely meet the qualifying standard; it sets the benchmark that others chase. In the Indian context, this means transforming from a cost-arbitrage-driven manufacturer into an **innovation-driven, quality-focused global player**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics of World-Class Status

| Metric | World-Class Benchmark | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | **≥ 85%** | Percentage |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | **≥ 99%** | Percentage |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | **≤ 3.4 (Six Sigma)** | DPMO |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | **≥ 98%** | Percentage |
| **Inventory Turns** | **≥ 12–15 per year** | Turns/Year |
| **Setup Time (SMED)** | **< 10 minutes** | Minutes |
| **Lead Time Reduction** | **Order-to-Delivery ≤ 5 days** | Days |
| **Scrap / Rework Cost** | **< 1% of sales** | Percentage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These metrics are **bolded** because they form the **measurable backbone** of WCM assessment. Any factory failing consistently on three or more metrics is categorised as a **"Below World-Class" (BWC)** operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Foundational Pillars of World Class Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> WCM is built on **five integrated pillars**. KTU examiners frequently test the interconnection between these pillars. Memorise this framework using the mnemonic **"Q-C-D-F-I"**.

### Pillar 1 — Quality (Total Quality Management)
- **Customer-centric definition** of quality (voice of customer → CTQs)
- Tools: **SPC, FMEA, Pareto, Ishikawa (Fishbone), 5-Why, Poka-Yoke, Taguchi Methods**
- Quality Guru lineage: **Deming → Juran → Crosby → Ishikawa → Feigenbaum**
- Cultural philosophy: **"Build quality in, do not inspect it in."**

### Pillar 2 — Cost (Lean Production & Activity-Based Costing)
- Elimination of **seven (or eight) wastes** (TIMWOOD + Skills underutilisation)
- **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** to identify non-value-added (NVA) activities
- Target: **30–50% cost reduction** across 3–5 years

### Pillar 3 — Delivery (Agile & Responsive Supply Chains)
- **JIT (Just-in-Time)** to compress inventory
- **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** and **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, Replenishment)**
- **OTIF (On Time In Full)** delivery metric

### Pillar 4 — Flexibility (Mass Customisation & Modular Design)
- **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)** for quick changeovers
- **Modular product architecture** (platform strategy)
- **Mixed-model assembly lines**

### Pillar 5 — Innovation (Continuous Improvement & Industry 4.0)
- **Kaizen (改善)**, **Kaikaku (改革)**, **Gemba walks**
- **Digital twins, IoT, AI/ML, additive manufacturing, cyber-physical systems**
- **Suggestion systems** — world-class target: **≥ 10 suggestions per employee per year**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Framework Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **critical numerical formulas** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in Part A and Part B answers. **Avoid using `|` characters** inside any cell to preserve table syntax.

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Boundary Condition / Benchmark | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overall Equipment Effectiveness | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $\text{OEE} \geq 85\%$ | Percentage |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{Planned Runtime} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Runtime}}$ | $A \geq 90\%$ | Percentage |
| Performance | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Units}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | $P \geq 95\%$ | Percentage |
| Quality | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | $Q \geq 99\%$ | Percentage |
| First Pass Yield | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units Passed First Time}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}}$ | $\text{FPY} \geq 99\%$ | Percentage |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | $n$ = number of process steps | Ratio |
| Sigma Level | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{2 \cdot \text{Std. Deviation}}$ | $6\sigma \Rightarrow 3.4$ DPMO | Sigma |
| Inventory Turns | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | $\text{IT} \geq 12$ | Turns/Year |
| Takt Time | $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | Drives line balancing | Seconds/Unit |
| Cost of Poor Quality | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Target: $< 2.5\%$ of sales | INR / USD |
| Productivity Index | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\text{Output Value}}{\text{Input Cost}}$ | Continuous improvement $\geq 5\%$/year | Ratio |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Sector (India)**: Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors — deploying **SMED + TPM** for global competitiveness.
- **Aerospace & Defence**: HAL, Tata Advanced Systems — adopting **Six Sigma + Digital Twins** under "Make in India".
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Sun Pharma, Dr. Reddy's — using **FDA-grade quality systems + WCM** for global exports.
- **Electronics (PLI-driven)**: Apple Foxconn, Dixon Technologies — leveraging **Lean + Industry 4.0** for export-grade output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: WCM is the **operational bridge** between a firm's corporate strategy (Porter / Ansoff) and its shop-floor execution. Without WCM, strategy remains a PowerPoint; with WCM, strategy becomes reality on the **Gemba** (shop floor).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework Derivations & Matrices

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hayes-Wheelwright Product-Process Matrix (Strategic Positioning)

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a management framework, KTU examiners treat the **Hayes-Wheelwright Matrix** as a derived analytical tool. The logical derivation is presented below.

**Step 1 — Define the Two Strategic Axes**

$$\text{Vertical Axis} = \text{Process Lifecycle Stage (one-of-four)}$$

$$\text{Horizontal Axis} = \text{Product Lifecycle Stage (one-of-four)}$$

**Step 2 — Stage Definitions**

$$\text{Product Stages} = \{\text{Introduction},\ \text{Growth},\ \text{Maturity},\ \text{Decline}\}$$

$$\text{Process Stages} = \{\text{Jumbled},\ \text{Disconnected},\ \text{Connected/Segmented},\ \text{Integrated Flow}\}$$

**Step 3 — Logical Mapping**

$$\text{Alignment Condition} = (\text{Product Stage}_i \leftrightarrow \text{Process Stage}_i)$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The matrix prescribes that **process capability must synchronise with product evolution**. Mismatches produce either **under-engineered processes** (wasted capability) or **over-engineered processes** (stranded cost).

$$\text{Strategic Outcome} = f(\text{Product Maturity},\ \text{Process Flow Integration})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip**: When the question asks "Discuss the role of process selection in manufacturing strategy," always map your answer on the **Hayes-Wheelwright matrix** — it is the **highest-weight analytical framework** in Module 4.

## 3.2 Derivation of Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)

**Step 1 — Decompose OEE into Three Orthogonal Factors**

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

**Step 2 — Express Each Factor Numerically**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\text{Loading Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Loading Time}} \\
P &= \dfrac{\text{Total Pieces Produced} \times \text{Ideal Cycle Time}}{\text{Operating Time}} \\
Q &= \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)**

A machine has:
- Planned Production Time = **480 minutes/day**
- Downtime (breakdowns + setup) = **60 minutes/day**
- Ideal Cycle Time = **1.0 minute/unit**
- Actual Units Produced = **380 units**
- Defective Units = **20 units**

**Compute A:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{480 - 60}{480} \\
  &= \dfrac{420}{480} \\
  &= 0.875 = 87.5\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute P:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{380 \times 1.0}{420} \\
  &= \dfrac{380}{420} \\
  &= 0.9048 = 90.48\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute Q:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{380 - 20}{380} \\
  &= \dfrac{360}{380} \\
  &= 0.9474 = 94.74\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute OEE:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} &= 0.875 \times 0.9048 \times 0.9474 \\
           &= 0.7500 = 75.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:**

$$\text{OEE} = 75\% \Rightarrow \text{World-Class Gap} = 85\% - 75\% = 10\%$$

This **10-point OEE gap** is a typical KTU-style diagnostic, implying the firm is **"Average" globally but not World Class**.

## 3.3 India Diagnostic Matrix — WCM Readiness Across Sectors

| Sector | WCM Maturity Level | Critical Gaps | Strategic Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Advanced (Tier-1 OEMs WCM-compliant) | EV transition, software-defined vehicles | Industry 4.0 + Battery Innovation |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | High (FDA-compliant) | API self-reliance, R&D depth | PLI Scheme + Biotech parks |
| **Electronics (Mobile)** | Emerging (Foxconn, Dixon ramp-up) | Component ecosystem, chip fabrication | PLI + Semiconductor Mission |
| **Textiles & Apparel** | Low to Medium | Productivity (≈ 1/3 of China), compliance | ATUFS + Technical Textiles |
| **Heavy Engineering** | Medium | Quality consistency, export orientation | Sub-contracting to global OEMs |
| **Defence Manufacturing** | Nascent | Indigenisation, advanced metallurgy | Make-in-India + iDEX |
| **Capital Goods** | Medium | R&D spend (< 1% revenue) | Capability building + PPP |

## 3.4 Schonberger's 17 (or 18) Principles — Conceptual Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> **Richard Schonberger** consolidated Japanese WCM principles into **17 customer-oriented principles** (later expanded to 18). For KTU answers, group them under **five themes** for higher marks.

$$\text{Schonberger Principles} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{5} T_i$$

Where the themes are:

- **T1 (Customer & Quality)**: Principle 1, 2 → Customer first, quality as the job.
- **T2 (Just-in-Time)**: Principles 3–6 → JIT, small lots, uniform workload.
- **T3 (Continuous Improvement)**: Principles 7–10 → Kaizen, preventive maintenance, low-cost automation.
- **T4 (Workforce)**: Principles 11–14 → Training, suggestor systems, worker autonomy.
- **T5 (Supplier Partnership)**: Principles 15–17 → Long-term relations, single sourcing, reduction of supplier base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: A 14-mark question on WCM that **maps Schonberger's principles** to the Indian context (e.g., to Maruti Suzuki or Tata Steel) consistently scores **13–14 marks** because it demonstrates applied synthesis.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define World Class Manufacturing (WCM). List any four of its defining characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

World Class Manufacturing is a state of operational excellence in which an enterprise deploys an **integrated system of best-in-class practices** (lean, TQM, TPM, Six Sigma, Kaizen) to achieve superior performance in **quality, cost, delivery, flexibility, and innovation**.

**Four Defining Characteristics:**
1. **Customer-driven quality** with zero-defect tolerance.
2. **Lean production systems** eliminating all forms of waste (TIMWOOD).
3. **High responsiveness** with lead times measured in days, not weeks.
4. **Continuous improvement culture** powered by empowered employees (≥ 10 suggestions/employee/year).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Writing only the definition without listing characteristics = **1/3 marks**. Always pair definition with **four measurable attributes**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: What is Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)? State the world-class benchmark value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

OEE is a composite metric that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilised, expressed as the product of three factors:

$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

The world-class benchmark for OEE is **≥ 85%** (broken down as A ≥ 90%, P ≥ 95%, Q ≥ 99%).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Students forget to **state all three sub-components** or omit the **85% benchmark**. Both omissions cost 1 mark each.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the concept of World Class Manufacturing. Discuss the role of TQM and JIT as its core enablers.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (2 Marks):**
WCM is an integrative manufacturing paradigm that combines the best global practices into a unified operational system. It demands excellence across the five performance dimensions — **Q-C-D-F-I**.

**TQM as a Core Enabler (2.5 Marks):**
- TQM (Total Quality Management) embeds **quality at the source**.
- Tools: SPC, FMEA, Poka-Yoke, Taguchi Loss Function.
- Philosophy shift from **inspection → prevention → built-in quality**.
- Statistical evidence: Six Sigma quality = **3.4 DPMO**.

**JIT as a Core Enabler (2.5 Marks):**
- JIT (Just-in-Time) synchronises **production with actual demand**, eliminating inventory buffers.
- Pull system driven by **Kanban signals**.
- Reduces **WIP inventory by 50–90%** and lead time by 70%.
- Enables rapid detection of defects (Jidoka principle).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Do not write TQM and JIT in isolation. Always show **how they interlink within WCM** (JIT exposes quality problems; TQM solves them). Skipping the linkage forfeits 1–2 marks.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the help of Schonberger's principles, discuss how Indian manufacturing firms can achieve WCM status.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Schonberger's 17 Principles (3 Marks):** Customer-first, JIT flow, small-lot production, uniform plant loading, quality at source, Kaizen, preventive maintenance, low-cost automation, vendor partnership, etc.

**Indian Context Application (4 Marks):**
- **Maruti Suzuki** → JIT pull system + supplier integration = benchmark WCM case in India.
- **Tata Steel (Jamshedpur)** → TPM + Kaizen yielding **OEE > 85%**.
- **Tata Motors (Pune)** → SMED + modular platforms.
- **Bharat Electronics** → Six Sigma deployment for defence electronics.
- **Wipro Infrastructure** → Lean office transformation.

Strategic alignment with **Make in India, PLI schemes, and Industry 4.0** accelerates WCM adoption in Indian MSMEs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Generic answers without **specific Indian company examples** lose 2–3 marks. KTU examiners reward **applied, India-specific case illustrations**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: What are the key performance metrics of a world-class manufacturing system? Compute the OEE for a machine with the following data: Planned Production Time = 600 min; Downtime = 90 min; Ideal Cycle Time = 0.8 min/unit; Total Units Produced = 540; Defective Units = 30.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Key Performance Metrics (3 Marks):** OEE, FPY, DPMO, Inventory Turns, OTD, Cost of Poor Quality, Takt Time, Sigma Level.

**Numerical Computation (4 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Loading Time} &= 600 - 90 = 510 \text{ min} \\
A &= \dfrac{510}{600} = 0.8500 = 85.00\% \\
P &= \dfrac{540 \times 0.8}{510} = \dfrac{432}{510} = 0.8471 = 84.71\% \\
Q &= \dfrac{540 - 30}{540} = \dfrac{510}{540} = 0.9444 = 94.44\% \\
\text{OEE} &= 0.8500 \times 0.8471 \times 0.9444 \\
           &= 0.6800 = 68.00\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** OEE = **68%** indicates **significant WCM gap** (target ≥ 85%); priority actions are **breakdown reduction (TPM)** and **quality improvement (Six Sigma)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Showing only the final OEE without **step-by-step A, P, Q calculations** forfeits 2 marks. Always **show intermediate values**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: "Make in India" initiative is the strategic vehicle for India to attain WCM status." Critically analyse this statement with reference to PLI schemes, infrastructure development, and Industry 4.0 adoption.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Strategic Vision (1.5 Marks):**
"Make in India" (launched **September 2014**) targets raising manufacturing's GDP share from **~16% to 25%** and creating **100 million jobs**.

**Strengths of the Argument (3 Marks):**
- **PLI (Production Linked Incentive) Schemes** across 14 sectors (₹1.97 lakh crore outlay) are attracting global OEMs (Apple, Samsung, Foxconn).
- **PM Gati Shakti** integrates 16 ministries for multimodal logistics infrastructure.
- **India Semiconductor Mission** with ₹76,000 crore outlay addresses the chip deficit.
- **NIP (National Infrastructure Pipeline)** of ₹111 lakh crore investment.
- **Industry 4.0 adoption** is accelerating in automotive, pharma, and FMCG sectors.

**Critical Concerns (2 Marks):**
- **MSME skill gap** — only ~10% of MSMEs practice formal quality systems.
- **R&D spend** at ~0.65% of GDP (vs. China 2.4%, South Korea 4.8%).
- **Ease of Doing Business** has improved but land acquisition and labour laws remain bottlenecks.
- **Export orientation** — still only ~2% of global merchandise trade.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
"Make in India" is a **necessary but not sufficient** condition for WCM. Success requires **simultaneous deep execution** of TQM, Lean, TPM, and Industry 4.0 at the **Gemba (shop-floor)** level.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall**: Purely promotional answers (all positives) or purely critical answers (all negatives) lose marks. KTU examiners expect a **balanced critical analysis** with **both strengths and weaknesses**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CARD — PRINT BEFORE EXAM**

- **WCM Definition** = Integrated excellence across Quality, Cost, Delivery, Flexibility, Innovation (**Q-C-D-F-I**).
- **OEE Formula** = $A \times P \times Q$ with world-class benchmark **≥ 85%**.
- **Six Sigma** = **3.4 DPMO**; **5S** is the foundation of all WCM deployments.
- **Lean vs. Six Sigma**: Lean removes **waste**; Six Sigma removes **variation**; WCM uses both.
- **Schonberger's principles** = **17 customer-oriented rules** grouped into 5 themes.
- **Hayes-Wheelwright matrix** aligns **product lifecycle stage** with **process capability stage**.
- **JIT** = Pull system, Kanban-driven, **inventory turns ≥ 12/year**.
- **TPM** targets **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents** (autonomous maintenance pillars).
- **Kaizen** = continuous small improvements; **Kaikaku** = radical transformation.
- **Indian Context**:
   - Manufacturing share of GDP ≈ **16–17%** (vs. China ~28%, South Korea ~29%).
   - PLI schemes cover **14 sectors**, total outlay ≈ **₹1.97 lakh crore**.
   - **Make in India** + **Atmanirbhar Bharat** = twin policy pillars.
   - **OEE in Indian industry** averages **~60%**, well below the 85% WCM benchmark.
- **Industry 4.0 pillars** in India: IoT, AI/ML, Digital Twins, Additive Manufacturing, Cobots.
- **MSME contribution**: ~**30% of GDP, 45% of exports, 110 million jobs** — critical for WCM diffusion.
- **World-class OTD** ≥ **98%**; **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** target ≤ **2.5% of sales**.
- **Benchmarking frameworks**: Deming Prize, EFQM, Malcolm Baldrige, Shingo Prize.
- **Future Frontiers**: **Industry 5.0** (human-centric + sustainable) and **Net-Zero Manufacturing**.
- **Gemba** = the real place where value is created — WCM lives or dies on the shop floor.
